Professor View 01 / 28
Business English -- 30 Aulas

Andreia Heins

De "eu só consigo ser eu em português" a ser ela mesma em inglês — em reuniões, congressos e apresentações de cirurgia robótica

B1 São Paulo, SP Enfermeira -- Cirurgia Robótica (Einstein) 60 min / Online
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Andreia Heins
36 anos
Enfermeira -- transição para Cirurgia Robótica (Hospital Albert Einstein)
São Paulo, SP
B1 (Intermediário)
Business English -- contexto médico-corporativo e cirurgia robótica
30 aulas de 60 min (2x/semana)
Online (Zoom)

Jornada de Transformação

De: "Eu só consigo ser eu em português" — travada na produção oral, falando tudo no presente, com o inglês enterrado depois de anos sem usar na fala

Para: "Ser eu em inglês" — comunicar-se com confiança em reuniões com a Medtronic e a Stryker, apresentar trabalhos em congressos internacionais e liderar discussões na LATAM, em 30 aulas

Promessa do Programa

De "nunca sair do intermediário" a "ser eu mesma em inglês" — em 30 aulas, Andreia vai desenterrar o inglês que já está dentro dela e levá-lo para reuniões, congressos e apresentações com a mesma confiança que ela tem em português.

Nas palavras dela: "Eu fiz inglês muitos anos na cultura inglesa e em outros lugares, mas eu nunca saí do intermediário." · "O inglês é o desenterrar — é tudo isso na minha cabeça."

Forças

  • Autoconsciência linguística precisa -- sabe exatamente onde trava
  • Base gramatical receptiva sólida -- 11-12 anos de estudo formal
  • Leitura em inglês funcional -- usou inglês acadêmico na faculdade
  • Motivação genuína e urgência real -- transição de carreira em curso
  • Comunicação direta e articulada -- alta facilidade verbal em português

Pontos de Melhoria

  • Produção oral travada -- uso exclusivo do presente na fala espontânea
  • Bloqueio de identidade em inglês -- "não consigo ser eu em inglês"
  • Tempos verbais do passado -- evita o passado ao falar ("Eu não falo no passado")
  • Listening em contexto técnico/profissional acelerado de nativos
  • Escrita profissional formal -- não praticada desde a faculdade

Currículo -- 30 Aulas

Três blocos: Aulas 1-10 desbloqueio da produção oral + ativação dos tempos verbais passivos · Aulas 11-20 inglês de reuniões, negociação e apresentação com vocabulário medtech · Aulas 21-30 simulações completas (congresso, call com fornecedor, pitch de projeto)

#TemaFoco LinguísticoAtividade PrincipalHomework
1Unlocking the English That's Already There -- Diagnostic & Professional IdentityPresent simple vs Past simple + Question formationDiagnostic conversation + self-introduction role-playRecord 3-min audio self-introduction
2My Career Story -- From the ICU to Robotic SurgeryPast simple (regular & irregular)Storytelling: career timelineRecord your career story (2 min)
3What I Do Every Day -- Routines & ResponsibilitiesPresent simple + adverbs of frequencyHot seat: daily routineWrite your weekly routine
4What's Happening Now -- The Transition in ProgressPresent continuous vs present simpleDescribe your current transitionRecord "what's changing for me"
5Talking About Experience -- Have You Ever...?Present perfect (experience)Q&A about professional experienceWrite 5 present-perfect sentences
6Connecting Past and PresentPresent perfect vs past simpleTimeline reconstructionRecord an experience summary
7Plans and Intentions -- Kickoff & Next StepsFuture: going to / willPlanning a project kickoffWrite next-quarter plans
8Describing People and TeamsComparatives + adjectivesDescribe your surgical teamRecord a team description
9Asking Smart Questions in MeetingsQuestion forms (all tenses)Meeting Q&A simulationWrite 8 meeting questions
10Block 1 Review -- My Professional IdentityIntegrated tensesFull self-presentation simulationRecord 3-min presentation
11Opening a Meeting -- Agendas & RolesPresent simple + meeting framesBoard meeting opener role-playWrite a meeting agenda
12Talking Numbers -- Data, Volumes & ResultsNumbers + quantifiers + data languagePresent surgical volume dataRecord a data summary
13Describing a Product -- The Da Vinci SystemPassive voice (present)Explain the robotic systemWrite a product description
14Explaining a Process -- How the Surgery WorksSequencing + passiveWalk through a procedureRecord a process explanation
15Agreeing & Disagreeing with SuppliersOpinion language + modal verbsNegotiation role-play (Medtronic)Write a position email
16Making Requests & ProposalsPolite forms: "Could I...", "Would you mind..."Request role-playWrite a proposal email
17Handling Problems & ComplaintsFirst conditional + modalsProblem-solving callRecord a complaint resolution
18Following Up -- Emails & Action ItemsReported speech + writingPost-meeting follow-upWrite a follow-up email
19Talking About Risks & SafetyModals of probabilityRisk discussionRecord a risk briefing
20Block 2 Review -- Leading a Supplier MeetingIntegrated meeting languageFull meeting simulationRecord a meeting summary
21Presenting with Confidence -- Structure & SignpostingPresentation languageMini-pitch (60 seconds)Record a 60-sec pitch
22Telling a Clinical Story -- Case PresentationPast tenses + narrativeCase presentationRecord a case story
23Q&A Under Pressure -- Handling QuestionsQuestion handling + hedgingHot seat Q&AWrite anticipated questions
24At an International Congress -- NetworkingSmall talk + social EnglishNetworking role-playRecord a networking introduction
25Chairing a Session -- Moderating DiscussionDiscourse markersSession moderationRecord a session opener
26The Sales Pitch -- Negotiating TermsConditionals + persuasionNegotiation simulationWrite a negotiation script
27Cross-Cultural Communication -- LATAM & the USRegister & politenessMulti-party callRecord a cultural reflection
28Defending Your Position -- ArgumentationAdvanced opinion languageDebate simulationRecord an argument
29The Full Presentation -- Congress TalkAll structures integrated10-min congress talkRecord a full talk
30Graduation Simulation -- Robotic Surgery SummitIntegrated immersionFull immersion simulationFinal reflection audio

Welcome, Andreia!

Onboarding

Bem-vinda ao programa! Antes de cada aula, complete as atividades de Pre-class. Aqui estão 5 frases de emergência que você pode usar a qualquer momento:

1 Could you repeat that, please? Poderia repetir, por favor?
2 I am not sure I understand. Could you explain? Não tenho certeza se entendi. Poderia explicar?
3 Let me think about that for a moment. Deixe-me pensar um momento.
4 That is a great question. Essa é uma ótima pergunta.
5 In my experience, I would say... Na minha experiência, eu diria...
Aula 01 -- Pre-class

Unlocking the English That's Already There

Diagnostic & Professional Identity: Present simple vs Past simple + Question formation
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Background -- the experience and training that you have
"My background is in nursing."
Responsible For -- having the duty to manage or take care of something
"I'm responsible for the robotic surgery program."
Deal With -- to take action to handle a person, task, or situation
"I deal with international suppliers like Medtronic and Stryker."
In Charge Of -- having control or command of something
"I'm in charge of coordinating the surgical team."
Transition Into -- to move from one role or stage to another
"I'm transitioning into robotic surgery."
Supplier -- a company that provides products or services
"Medtronic is one of our main suppliers."
Robotic Surgery -- an operation performed using robotic systems
"I'm transitioning into robotic surgery at Einstein."
Stakeholder -- a person with an interest or concern in a project
"The main stakeholders are the surgeons and the suppliers."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Background
Responsible For
Deal With
In Charge Of
Transition Into
Supplier
Robotic Surgery
Stakeholder

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Andreia is a nurse. Her background is in nursing, and she works at Hospital Albert Einstein in São Paulo. For many years, she worked in the ICU and in neurology. Last year, she started a new chapter: she is transitioning into robotic surgery. Now she is responsible for the robotic surgery program, and she deals with international suppliers like Medtronic and Stryker. Yesterday, she met the Medtronic team and discussed the new system. She is in charge of coordinating the surgical team, and the main stakeholders are the surgeons and the suppliers. She studied English for many years, but now she practices speaking every day.

1. Where does Andreia work now?
A She worked in the ICU and in neurology.
B She works at Hospital Albert Einstein.
C She studied English for many years.
2. Which sentence describes a finished action in the past?
A She deals with suppliers like Medtronic.
B Yesterday, she met the Medtronic team and discussed the new system.
C She is responsible for the robotic surgery program.
3. Who are the main stakeholders?
A The surgeons and the suppliers.
B The ICU and the neurology team.
C The universities in São Paulo.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Simple = facts, routines, things that are true now (fatos, rotinas, o que é verdade agora)
Past Simple = finished actions in the past (ações terminadas no passado — com "yesterday", "last year", "in 2010")

TenseStructureExample
Present SimpleSubject + verb (base/s)"I work at Einstein."
Past SimpleSubject + verb (-ed / irregular)"Yesterday I worked with Medtronic."
QuestionsWh- + do/does/did + subject + verb"What do you do?" / "Who did you meet?"

Common Mistake:

"Yesterday I work with Medtronic."

"Yesterday I worked with Medtronic."

When the action is finished and in the past, use the past simple. Esse é o ponto que mais trava na fala — o passado é seu foco principal nesta aula.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or verb form.

"My is in nursing."
"Last year, I into robotic surgery."
"I with suppliers like Medtronic and Stryker."
"Yesterday, I the Medtronic team."
"I am for the robotic surgery program."
"The main are the surgeons and the suppliers."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "I currently work at Hospital Albert Einstein."
? "Hi, everyone. Let me introduce myself."
? "I deal with suppliers like Medtronic and Stryker."
? "I'm transitioning into robotic surgery."
? "My name is Andreia, and my background is in nursing."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

My background is in nursing, and I currently work at Hospital Albert Einstein.
I'm responsible for the robotic surgery program.
Yesterday, I worked with the Medtronic team and discussed the new system.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You are on a kickoff call with Medtronic. A representative asks: "What do you do?" What is the best response?
A "I am Andreia. I like surgery."
B "I'm in charge of the robotic surgery program, and I deal with suppliers like Medtronic."
C "I have a job at a hospital in São Paulo."
2. A colleague asks: "What did you do yesterday?" What is the correct answer?
A "Yesterday I meet the Medtronic team."
B "Yesterday I work with the Medtronic team."
C "Yesterday I met the Medtronic team and discussed the new system."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are on a kickoff call with Medtronic. They ask: "Could you introduce yourself?" Record your answer in English. Try to include: your name, your background, your current role, and your transition into robotic surgery.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1 Hi, I'm Andreia. My background is in nursing. Oi, sou a Andreia. Minha formação é em enfermagem.
2 I currently work at Hospital Albert Einstein. Atualmente trabalho no Hospital Albert Einstein.
3 I'm responsible for the robotic surgery program. Sou responsável pelo programa de cirurgia robótica.
4 I'm transitioning into surgical robotics. Estou fazendo a transição para a robótica cirúrgica.
5 I deal with suppliers like Medtronic and Stryker. Lido com fornecedores como Medtronic e Stryker.
Aula 02 -- Pre-class

My Career Story

From the ICU to Robotic Surgery: Past Simple (regular & irregular)
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Graduate From -- to finish your studies at a school or university
"I graduated from nursing school in 2008."
Specialize In -- to focus on one particular area of work
"I specialized in intensive care."
Get Promoted -- to move to a higher position at work
"I got promoted to coordinator."
Achievement -- something good that you did with effort
"My biggest achievement was opening the robotic surgery program."
Challenge -- a difficult task or situation
"The hardest challenge was learning a new system."
Lead -- to be in front of and guide a team (past: led)
"I led a small team during the pandemic."
Milestone -- an important stage or event in your career
"A key milestone was my move into robotic surgery."
Shift -- a fixed period of working time, like the night shift
"I worked the night shift for several years."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Graduate From
Specialize In
Get Promoted
Achievement
Challenge
Lead
Milestone
Shift

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Andreia graduated from nursing school in 2008. She started her career in the ICU, where she worked the night shift for six years. She specialized in intensive care and later moved to neurology. During the pandemic, she led a small team and got promoted to coordinator. It was a difficult challenge, but it became her biggest achievement. Last year, she began a new milestone: she started her transition into robotic surgery.

1. When did Andreia graduate from nursing school?
A In 2008.
B Last year.
C During the pandemic.
2. Which verb is an irregular past form?
A worked
B led
C specialized
3. What was her biggest achievement?
A Working the night shift.
B Leading the team through a difficult challenge during the pandemic.
C Graduating in 2008.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Past Simple = finished actions in the past (ações terminadas — com "in 2008", "last year", "for six years", "ago").
Regular verbs add -ed. Irregular verbs change form (você precisa memorizá-los).

TypeBase → PastExample
Regular (-ed)work → worked"I worked the night shift."
Irregularlead → led · get → got · begin → began"I led the team."
Questionsdid + base verb"What did you do before?"

Common Mistake:

"I begin my career in the ICU."

"I began my career in the ICU."

Begin → began (irregular). Ação terminada no passado pede o passado — não use o presente.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct past form.

"I from nursing school in 2008."
"I a small team during the pandemic."
"I the night shift for six years."
"I promoted to coordinator."
"I in intensive care."
"Last year, I my transition into robotic surgery."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "During the pandemic, I led a small team and got promoted."
? "I graduated from nursing school in 2008."
? "Last year, I began my transition into robotic surgery."
? "I started in the ICU and specialized in intensive care."
? "Now I deal with suppliers like Medtronic and Stryker."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

I graduated from nursing school and specialized in intensive care.
I worked the night shift for six years in the ICU.
I led a small team during the pandemic and got promoted.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. A colleague asks: "Where did you start your career?" What is the best answer?
A "I start in the ICU in 2008."
B "I started in the ICU, and I worked the night shift for six years."
C "I am starting in the ICU."
2. Someone asks: "How did you become a coordinator?" What is correct?
A "I leaded a team and get promoted."
B "I led a team during the pandemic and got promoted."
C "I lead a team and got promoted."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Tell your real career story in English. Record your answer. Include: where and when you graduated, where you worked, one challenge, and one achievement. Use the past simple.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1 I graduated from nursing school in 2008. Me formei em enfermagem em 2008.
2 I specialized in intensive care. Me especializei em terapia intensiva.
3 I worked in the ICU for several years. Trabalhei na UTI por vários anos.
4 I led a small team during the pandemic. Liderei uma pequena equipe durante a pandemia.
5 Last year, I started my transition into robotic surgery. No ano passado, comecei minha transição para a cirurgia robótica.
Aula 03 -- Pre-class

What I Do Every Day

Routines & Responsibilities: Present Simple + adverbs of frequency
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Routine -- the usual things you do regularly
"My routine starts at seven a.m."
Schedule -- a plan of when things happen
"I always check my schedule first."
Coordinate -- to organize people or activities to work well together
"I coordinate the surgical team every day."
Monitor -- to watch and check something regularly
"I monitor the equipment before surgery."
Report To -- to give information to a manager, or be managed by them
"I report to the medical director."
Task -- a piece of work you have to do
"My first task is the morning briefing."
Priority -- the most important thing to do first
"Safety is always my top priority."
Update -- new information about progress
"I often give updates to the team."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Routine
Schedule
Coordinate
Monitor
Report To
Task
Priority
Update

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Andreia's day starts early. She always checks her schedule first. She usually coordinates the surgical team in the morning, and she often monitors the equipment before every surgery. She reports to the medical director and regularly gives her updates. Safety is always her top priority, so the team never skips the safety check. She sometimes deals with suppliers in the afternoon, but her first task is always the morning briefing.

1. What does Andreia always do first?
A She coordinates the team.
B She checks her schedule.
C She deals with suppliers.
2. Where does the adverb "always" go?
A After the main verb.
B Before the main verb (and after "to be").
C At the end of the sentence.
3. What is her top priority?
A Safety.
B The afternoon briefing.
C Dealing with suppliers.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Simple = routines, habits, facts. Add -s for he/she/it.
Adverbs of frequency (always 100% → never 0%) go before the main verb, but after "to be".

RulePatternExample
Before main verbsubject + adverb + verb"I always check my schedule."
After "to be"subject + be + adverb"Safety is always my priority."
He/she/itverb + -s"She coordinates the team."

Common Mistake:

"I check always my schedule."

"I always check my schedule."

O advérbio de frequência vem ANTES do verbo principal.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word.

"My starts at seven a.m."
"I always check my first."
"I the surgical team every day."
"I to the medical director."
"Safety is always my top ."
"I often give to the team."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the words in the correct order (frequency adverb before the verb).

? check
? I
? my schedule
? always
? in the morning

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

My routine starts at seven, and I always check my schedule first.
I usually coordinate the team and monitor the equipment before surgery.
I report to the medical director, and safety is always my priority.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. A new team member asks: "What do you do first every day?" What is the best answer?
A "I check always my schedule."
B "I always check my schedule and coordinate the team."
C "I checking my schedule."
2. Someone asks: "Who does Andreia report to?" What is correct?
A "She report to the medical director."
B "She reports to the medical director."
C "She reporting to the medical director."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Describe your real daily routine in English. Record your answer. Include: what time you start, what you always do, what you usually do, and your top priority. Use adverbs of frequency.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1 My routine starts at seven a.m. Minha rotina começa às sete da manhã.
2 I always check my schedule first. Sempre confiro minha agenda primeiro.
3 I coordinate the surgical team every day. Coordeno a equipe cirúrgica todos os dias.
4 I report to the medical director. Reporto-me ao diretor médico.
5 Safety is always my top priority. A segurança é sempre minha prioridade máxima.
Aula 04 -- Pre-class

What's Happening Now

The Transition in Progress: Present Continuous vs Present Simple
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

These are the key words for this lesson. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Implement -- to start using a new system or plan
"Right now, we are implementing the Da Vinci system."
Train On -- to learn how to use something new
"I am training on the new robot this month."
Roll Out -- to introduce something new, step by step
"We are rolling out the robotic surgery program."
Adapt To -- to change in order to fit a new situation
"I am adapting to my new role."
Set Up -- to prepare or install something new
"We are setting up the new lab."
Progress -- forward movement or development
"The program is making good progress."
Pilot -- a small test before the full launch
"We are running a pilot first."
Expand -- to grow or become bigger
"The team is expanding."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Implement
Train On
Roll Out
Adapt To
Set Up
Progress
Pilot
Expand

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Andreia usually works in the surgery department, but right now she is going through a big change. This month, she is training on the new robot, and her team is implementing the Da Vinci system. They are running a pilot first, and they are setting up a new lab. The program is expanding, and everyone is adapting to the new routine. She still checks her schedule every morning, but these days her main focus is the rollout.

1. What is Andreia doing this month?
A She works in the surgery department.
B She is training on the new robot.
C She checks her schedule.
2. Which sentence describes a routine (not a temporary action now)?
A The team is implementing the system.
B She checks her schedule every morning.
C They are setting up a new lab.
3. Why does the text use "is expanding" and not "expands"?
A Because it is a change happening now, in progress.
B Because it is a permanent fact.
C Because it happened in the past.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Simple = routines, facts (every day, usually).
Present Continuous = am/is/are + verb-ing, for actions happening now or temporary/changing situations (now, right now, at the moment, currently, these days, this week/month).

TenseUseExample
Present Simpleroutine / fact"I work in surgery."
Present Continuousnow / temporary / changing"I am training this month."
Questionsam/is/are + subject + -ing"What are you working on now?"

Common Mistake:

"At the moment, I adapt to the new role."

"At the moment, I am adapting to the new role."

Situação em andamento agora → present continuous (am/is/are + -ing).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form.

"Right now, we are the Da Vinci system."
"I am on the new robot this month."
"I usually in the surgery department."
"At the moment, I am to my new role."
"We are a pilot first."
"The program is these days."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the words in the correct order (present continuous sentence).

? implementing
? We
? the Da Vinci system
? are
? right now

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself. Compare word by word.

Right now, we are implementing the Da Vinci system.
I am training on the new robot, and the team is adapting quickly.
We are running a pilot, and the program is making good progress.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. Brian asks: "What are you working on right now?" What is the best answer?
A "I implement the Da Vinci system."
B "Right now, I am implementing the Da Vinci system and training the team."
C "I am implement the Da Vinci system."
2. Which sentence is correct for a routine?
A "Every morning, I am checking my schedule."
B "Every morning, I check my schedule."
C "Every morning, I checking my schedule."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
What is happening in your work right now? Record your answer. Include: what you usually do (routine) and what you are doing these days (the transition). Use both present simple and present continuous.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1 Right now, we are implementing the Da Vinci system. Agora mesmo, estamos implementando o sistema Da Vinci.
2 I am training on the new robot this month. Estou treinando no novo robô este mês.
3 We are rolling out the robotic surgery program. Estamos implantando o programa de cirurgia robótica.
4 I am adapting to my new role. Estou me adaptando ao meu novo cargo.
5 The program is making good progress. O programa está progredindo bem.
Aula 05 -- Pre-class

Talking About Experience -- Have You Ever...?

Present perfect para experiência profissional: procedure, certification, simulation, mentor, hands-on, milestone, supervise, cutting-edge. Estruturas: "Have you ever...?", "I have + particípio (+ número)", "I have never...". Missão: apresentar sua experiência para o Dr. Adams.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Procedure -- a medical operation or formal process
"I have assisted in fifty robotic procedures." (Procedimento)
Certification -- an official document that proves your skill
"I have completed two international certifications." (Certificação)
Simulation -- a safe practice of a real situation
"New nurses start with simulations." (Simulação)
Mentor -- an experienced person who guides you
"Dr. Ferraz has been my mentor for years."
Hands-on -- practical, doing the real work yourself
"The training is very hands-on." (Prático / mão na massa)
Milestone -- an important moment of progress
"My first robotic case was a milestone." (Marco)
Supervise -- to watch and guide other people's work
"I have supervised many simulation sessions."
Cutting-edge -- the most modern and advanced of its kind
"Robotic surgery is cutting-edge technology." (De ponta)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its meaning.

Procedure
Certification
Simulation
Milestone
Supervise
Cutting-edge

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Dr. Adams visits Hospital Albert Einstein on Friday. He asks Andreia, "Have you ever worked with the newest console?" She smiles: "Yes, I have. I have assisted in more than fifty robotic procedures, and I have supervised many simulation sessions. I have completed two international certifications — but I have never presented my work abroad. Yet!"

1. Como o Dr. Adams pergunta sobre a experiência? / How does Dr. Adams ask about experience?
A "Did you ever worked with the console?"
B "Have you ever worked with the console?"
C "Are you ever working the console?"
2. Quantos procedimentos a Andreia já acompanhou? / How many procedures has Andreia assisted in?
A More than fifty.
B Two.
C One hundred and twenty.
3. O que ela NUNCA fez? / What has she never done?
A She has never supervised a simulation.
B She has never presented her work abroad.
C She has never completed a certification.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

GRAMMAR

Present perfect para experiência: have/has + particípio. A carreira inteira, sem data. / Your whole career, no specific date.

Structure / EstruturaUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
Have you ever + particípio?Perguntar sobre a carreira todaHave you ever supervised a team?
I have + particípio (+ número)Sua experiência, com númerosI have completed two certifications.
I have never + particípioNunca, até agoraI have never presented abroad.
Data específica? Past simple!Em 2019, ontem, na sexta...I completed the course in 2023.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Have you assisted in a robotic procedure?"
"I completed two certifications."
"I have operated the console alone."
"She supervised many sessions."
"The training is very ."
"My first robotic case was a real ."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Listen first, then put Friday's visit in the correct order.

?She presents her numbers: procedures and certifications.
?Dr. Adams arrives at Einstein on Friday.
?He invites her to the exchange program.
?He asks: "Have you ever worked with the newest console?"
?She says what she has never done — yet.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Pratique as frases da sua entrevista de sexta-feira.

I have assisted in more than fifty robotic procedures.
Já acompanhei mais de cinquenta procedimentos robóticos.
I have completed two international certifications.
Já concluí duas certificações internacionais.
I have never presented my work abroad.
Nunca apresentei meu trabalho no exterior.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each situation.

O Dr. Adams pergunta sobre sua experiência com o console. / Dr. Adams asks about your console experience.
A "Yes, I have. I have assisted in more than fifty procedures."
B "Yes, I assist fifty procedures in my life."
C "Yes, I am assisting fifty procedures yesterday."
Ele pergunta se você já apresentou no exterior (nunca apresentou). / He asks if you have presented abroad (you never have).
A "I not presented abroad."
B "I have never presented abroad — yet!"
C "I never have present abroad."
Ele pergunta QUANDO você concluiu a certificação. / He asks WHEN you completed the certification.
A "I have completed it in 2023."
B "I completed it in 2023."
C "I complete it in 2023."
Você quer perguntar sobre a carreira DELE. / You want to ask about HIS career.
A "Did you ever trained a team?"
B "Have you ever trained an international team?"
C "Have you ever train a team?"

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection

No right or wrong here. Think in English. Tap the mic and speak freely.

Present your experience to Dr. Adams in 5 sentences with the present perfect: what you have done (with numbers!), what you have completed, who has been your mentor, and one thing you have never done — yet. Use: I have assisted in..., I have completed..., I have supervised..., I have never...

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1Have you ever worked with robotic surgery?Você já trabalhou com cirurgia robótica?
2I have assisted in more than fifty procedures.Já acompanhei mais de cinquenta procedimentos.
3I have completed two international certifications.Já concluí duas certificações internacionais.
4I have never presented my work abroad.Nunca apresentei meu trabalho no exterior.
5My first robotic case was a real milestone.Meu primeiro caso robótico foi um verdadeiro marco.
Aula 06 -- Pre-class

Connecting Past and Present -- Your Career Timeline

Present perfect vs past simple: when the time is finished (I started in 2016) and when it connects to now (I have worked since 2018), plus since and for. New vocabulary: recently, so far, lately, ago, turning point, breakthrough, decade, throughout, gradually, look back on.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Recently -- a short time before now, in the recent past
"Recently I have trained a whole new team." (Recentemente)
So far -- up to this point in time, until now
"So far I have assisted in hundreds of procedures." (Até agora)
Lately -- during the recent period of time up to now
"Lately I have started to mentor younger nurses." (Ultimamente)
Ago -- a point in the past, measured back from now
"I started in the ICU more than ten years ago." (Atrás)
Turning point -- a moment of important change in a story or career
"Joining the surgical center was a turning point." (Ponto de virada)
Breakthrough -- an important development or success after hard work
"Training the team alone was a real breakthrough." (Grande avanço)
Decade -- a period of ten years
"For almost a decade I worked in intensive care." (Década)
Throughout -- during the whole of a period of time
"Throughout my career I have kept learning." (Ao longo de)
Gradually -- slowly and steadily, little by little
"I moved gradually from the ICU into surgery." (Gradualmente)
Look back on -- to think about a time or event in the past
"When I look back on my path, I feel proud." (Relembrar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Recently
So far
Lately
Ago
Turning point
Breakthrough
Decade
Throughout
Gradually
Look back on

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Andreia graduated as a nurse and started in the intensive care unit more than ten years ago. For almost a decade she worked there. In 2016 she moved into the surgical center, and that was a real turning point. Since 2018 she has worked with robotic surgery, and throughout this journey she has kept learning. Recently she has trained the first full robotic team, which was a real breakthrough. So far she has assisted in more than five hundred procedures, and lately she has started to mentor younger nurses.

1. Which sentence is in the past simple?
A "In 2016 she moved into the surgical center."
B "She has worked with robotic surgery since 2018."
C "She has trained the first robotic team."
2. Why is "she has worked with robotic surgery since 2018" in the present perfect?
A Because it started in 2018 and still continues now.
B Because it happened once and finished.
C Because it will happen next year.
3. What was Andreia's turning point?
A Moving into the surgical center in 2016.
B Graduating as a nurse.
C Mentoring younger nurses.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Simple vs Present Perfect

GRAMMAR

Usado para conectar o passado terminado com o que continua até agora. / Used to connect finished past time with what still continues now.

Form / FormaMeaning / SentidoExample / Exemplo
verb + ed / irregularPassado simples: tempo terminado / Past simple: finished timeI started in the ICU ten years ago.
have / has + past participlePresente perfeito: ligado ao agora / Present perfect: connected to nowI have worked here since 2018.
since + ponto no tempoInício de um período que continua / Start of a period that continuesI have been a nurse since 2010.
for + duraçãoQuanto tempo durou / How long it has lastedI have worked here for a decade.

Key point / Ponto-chave: com tempo terminado (ago, in 2016, last year) use o passado simples. Com ação que continua ou experiência geral (since, for, so far, recently) use o presente perfeito. Nunca "I have started ten years ago".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

" I have trained a whole new team."
" I have assisted in hundreds of procedures."
" I have started to mentor younger nurses."
"Joining the surgical center was a real ."
"For almost a I worked in intensive care."
" my career I have kept learning."

Stage 2: Put the Timeline in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the career events in the correct order.

?In 2018 she began to work with robotic surgery.
?Andreia graduated and started in the intensive care unit.
?Recently she has trained a new team and started to mentor nurses.
?A few years later she moved into the surgical center.
?Since then she has assisted in hundreds of procedures.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Since 2018 I have worked with robotic surgery.
Desde 2018 eu trabalho com cirurgia robótica.
So far I have assisted in hundreds of procedures.
Até agora eu auxiliei em centenas de procedimentos.
I started in the ICU more than ten years ago.
Eu comecei na UTI há mais de dez anos.
When I look back on my path, I feel proud.
Quando relembro minha trajetória, sinto orgulho.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

You want to say when you began your career, with a finished time. How do you say it?
A "I have started my career ten years ago."
B "I started my career ten years ago."
C "I start my career ten years ago."
You want to describe robotic work that began in 2018 and still continues. The best sentence is:
A "I have worked with robotic surgery since 2018."
B "I worked with robotic surgery since 2018."
C "I work with robotic surgery since 2018."
You want to say how long you have lasted in your role. Which word follows "have worked here"?
A "since a decade."
B "for a decade."
C "ago a decade."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Tell your career story in one minute. Start with the past (what you did and when, using the past simple) and connect it to the present (what you have done since then, using the present perfect with since, for, recently, and so far).

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1I started my career more than ten years ago.Comecei minha carreira há mais de dez anos.
2Since 2018 I have worked with robotic surgery.Desde 2018 trabalho com cirurgia robótica.
3So far I have assisted in hundreds of procedures.Até agora auxiliei em centenas de procedimentos.
4Recently I have trained a whole new team.Recentemente treinei uma equipe totalmente nova.
5When I look back on my path, I feel proud.Quando relembro minha trajetória, sinto orgulho.
Aula 07 -- Pre-class

Plans and Intentions -- Your Project Kickoff

The future with going to (decided plans: I am going to launch the kickoff) and will (predictions, offers, and promises: I think it will work, I will help you). New vocabulary: kickoff, upcoming, deadline, goal, forecast, roadmap, outcome, commit to, estimate, allocate.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Kickoff -- the official start of a project or plan
"The committee is going to launch the kickoff next quarter." (Início do projeto)
Upcoming -- happening soon, in the near future
"I am going to share our plan for the upcoming program." (Próximo)
Deadline -- the final date or time to finish something
"The deadline for the first phase is the end of March." (Prazo)
Goal -- something you plan and aim to achieve
"Our main goal is to train forty nurses in two years." (Meta)
Forecast -- a statement about what will probably happen
"I forecast that the first group will be ready by December." (Previsão)
Roadmap -- a plan that shows the main stages ahead
"Let me walk you through the roadmap for the program." (Plano de etapas)
Outcome -- the final result of a plan or action
"I really believe the outcome will be excellent." (Resultado)
Commit to -- to promise firmly to do something
"I commit to supporting each of you at every step." (Comprometer-se)
Estimate -- to calculate an approximate number or time
"I estimate that each nurse will need twenty hours." (Estimar)
Allocate -- to give time, money, or people to a task
"I am going to allocate one mentor to every two nurses." (Alocar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Kickoff
Upcoming
Deadline
Goal
Forecast
Roadmap
Outcome
Commit to
Estimate
Allocate

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Andreia is preparing the kickoff of an upcoming robotic training program. "We are going to launch it next quarter," she says, "and our main goal is to train forty nurses." She walks the team through the roadmap: the deadline for the first phase is the end of March. "I am going to allocate one mentor to every two learners," she adds. Then she makes a prediction: "I forecast that the outcome will be excellent, and I think the first group will be ready by December." Finally she promises, "I commit to support every nurse, and I will send the next steps after the meeting."

1. Which sentence is a plan she has already decided?
A "We are going to launch it next quarter."
B "I think the first group will be ready by December."
C "I will send the next steps after the meeting."
2. Why does she use "will" in "the outcome will be excellent"?
A Because it is a prediction or opinion about the future.
B Because it is a plan already on the calendar.
C Because it already happened.
3. What is the deadline for the first phase?
A The end of March.
B Next December.
C Two years from now.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- going to vs will

GRAMMAR

Usado para falar do futuro: planos já decididos e previsões ou decisões do momento. / Used to talk about the future: decided plans, and predictions or decisions made at the moment.

Form / FormaMeaning / SentidoExample / Exemplo
am / is / are + going to + verbPlano já decidido / A plan already decidedWe are going to launch the kickoff.
going to (with evidence)Previsão com sinal visível agora / Prediction with present evidenceLook at the budget -- it is going to be tight.
will + base verbPrevisão ou opinião / Prediction or opinionI think the outcome will be excellent.
will + base verbDecisão do momento, oferta, promessa / On-the-spot decision, offer, promiseDo not worry, I will send it today.

Key point / Ponto-chave: plano já decidido = going to. Previsão, oferta, promessa ou decisão feita na hora = will. Depois de will, use o verbo na base -- nunca "will to".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We are going to launch the next quarter."
"The for the first phase is the end of March."
"Our main is to train forty nurses."
"I that the first group will be ready by December."
"Let me walk you through the for the program."
"I am going to one mentor to every two nurses."

Stage 2: Put the Project Plan in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the project steps in the correct order.

?Andreia is going to allocate one mentor to every two nurses.
?The committee is going to set the main goal for the program.
?The first nurses will be ready for live procedures.
?The team will agree on the roadmap and the deadlines.
?The group will start the practice on the simulators.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

We are going to launch the kickoff next quarter.
Vamos lançar o início do projeto no próximo trimestre.
I forecast the outcome will be excellent.
Eu prevejo que o resultado será excelente.
Our main goal is to train forty nurses.
Nossa meta principal é treinar quarenta enfermeiros.
I commit to supporting each of you at every step.
Eu me comprometo a apoiar cada um de vocês em cada etapa.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

You want to talk about a plan you have already decided. The best sentence is:
A "We are going to launch the program next quarter."
B "We will launch the program next quarter, I just decided."
C "We launch the program next quarter."
You want to give an opinion or prediction about the result. Which is best?
A "I think the outcome will be excellent."
B "I think the outcome going to be excellent."
C "I think the outcome is excellent next year."
After "will", which form of the verb is correct?
A "I will to send the budget."
B "I will send the budget."
C "I will sending the budget."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Present a project you want to start at work in one minute. Talk about your decided plans (using going to), then forecast the results and make a promise (using will). Mention your goal, the deadline, and your next steps.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1We are going to launch the kickoff next quarter.Vamos lançar o início do projeto no próximo trimestre.
2Our main goal is to train forty nurses.Nossa meta principal é treinar quarenta enfermeiros.
3I forecast the outcome will be excellent.Eu prevejo que o resultado será excelente.
4Do not worry, I will send you the roadmap today.Não se preocupe, vou enviar o plano de etapas hoje.
5I commit to supporting each of you at every step.Eu me comprometo a apoiar cada um de vocês em cada etapa.
Aula 08 -- Pre-class

Describing People and Teams -- Your Surgical Team

Comparatives to compare people (She is more skilled than me, He is calmer than the others) and superlatives (the most reliable, the calmest). New vocabulary: reliable, skilled, supportive, confident, efficient, calm, detail-oriented, experienced, hardworking, easygoing.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Reliable -- someone you can always trust to do their job
"My scrub nurse is the most reliable person on the team." (Confiável)
Skilled -- very good at a particular job or task
"She is more skilled than me with the robotic arms." (Habilidoso)
Supportive -- ready to help and encourage other people
"A good leader is supportive when the team feels nervous." (Solidário)
Confident -- sure about your own ability
"He is more confident now than in his first year." (Confiante)
Efficient -- working well and fast, without wasting time
"The new schedule made our team more efficient." (Eficiente)
Calm -- relaxed and not nervous, even under pressure
"In an emergency, she is calmer than anyone else." (Calmo)
Detail-oriented -- careful about small but important things
"A detail-oriented nurse checks every instrument twice." (Atento aos detalhes)
Experienced -- someone who has done something for a long time
"He is more experienced than the rest of the team." (Experiente)
Hardworking -- putting a lot of effort into the work
"My team is hardworking and never gives up." (Esforçado)
Easygoing -- relaxed and pleasant to work with
"Our new resident is friendly and easygoing." (Tranquilo)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Reliable
Skilled
Supportive
Confident
Efficient
Calm
Detail-oriented
Experienced
Hardworking
Easygoing

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Andreia is describing her surgical team. "Mariana, my scrub nurse, is the most detail-oriented person I know," she says. "She is more reliable than anyone on the floor." Pedro is the youngest resident. "He is less experienced than the rest of us, but he is incredibly hardworking, and he is more confident every week." Carla, the anesthesiologist, is the calmest member of the team. "In an emergency, nobody stays calmer than her." Andreia adds, "I am more efficient when I work with them, because they are supportive and easygoing. Some colleagues are more skilled than me with the new robotic arms, and that is good, because I learn from them."

1. Who is the most detail-oriented person on the team?
A Mariana, the scrub nurse.
B Pedro, the resident.
C Carla, the anesthesiologist.
2. Why do we say "less experienced than" about Pedro?
A Because he has worked for a shorter time than the others.
B Because he is the oldest on the team.
C Because he is the most skilled.
3. How does Andreia describe Carla under pressure?
A As the calmest member, because nobody stays calmer than her.
B As the least reliable member of the team.
C As more nervous than the others.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comparatives & Superlatives

GRAMMAR

Usado para comparar pessoas e equipes. / Used to compare people and teams.

Form / FormaRule / RegraExample / Exemplo
Short adjective + er + than1 sílaba: + er + thanShe is calmer than me.
Adjective ending in -yy vira i + er: easy → easier, busy → busierToday is busier than yesterday.
more + long adjective + than2+ sílabas: more ... thanHe is more reliable than the others.
the + est / the most + adjectiveSuperlativo: o mais de todosShe is the most detail-oriented.
Irregulargood → better, bad → worse, far → furtherTeamwork is better than working alone.

Key point / Ponto-chave: nunca use "more" com adjetivo curto que já tem -er. Não diga "more calmer" nem "more better" -- diga apenas calmer e better. E não esqueça o than ao comparar duas pessoas.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"My scrub nurse is the most person on the team."
"In an emergency, Carla is than anyone else."
"Pedro is less than the rest of us."
"She is more than me with the robotic arms."
"The new schedule made our team more ."
"A good leader is when the team feels nervous."

Stage 2: Rank the Team by Experience

Order

Listen first, then put the team in order from the most experienced to the least experienced.

?Mariana, the scrub nurse, has worked here for six years.
?Carla is the most experienced surgeon in the room.
?Lucas, the intern, started last month and is the least experienced.
?Andreia comes next, with twelve years in the operating room.
?Pedro, the resident, is newer but learns faster than anyone.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

She is more reliable than anyone on the team.
Ela é mais confiável do que qualquer um da equipe.
In an emergency, nobody stays calmer than her.
Numa emergência, ninguém fica mais calmo do que ela.
He is more confident now than in his first year.
Ele está mais confiante agora do que no primeiro ano.
My team is hardworking, supportive, and easygoing.
Minha equipe é esforçada, solidária e tranquila.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to compare two colleagues using a short adjective. Which is correct?
A "Mariana is calmer than Pedro."
B "Mariana is more calmer than Pedro."
C "Mariana is calm than Pedro."
You want to compare two people with a long adjective. Which is best?
A "She is more experienced than me."
B "She is experienceder than me."
C "She is more experienced me."
Which sentence uses the irregular comparative correctly?
A "Teamwork is more better than working alone."
B "Teamwork is better than working alone."
C "Teamwork is gooder than working alone."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe your real team in one minute. Compare three colleagues using comparatives (more skilled than, calmer than, more reliable than) and one superlative (the most ..., the calmest). Say what makes each one special and what you learn from them.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1She is more reliable than anyone on the team.Ela é mais confiável do que qualquer um da equipe.
2He is less experienced, but he learns faster than anyone.Ele é menos experiente, mas aprende mais rápido do que todos.
3Carla is the calmest member of the team.Carla é a integrante mais calma da equipe.
4My team is hardworking, supportive, and easygoing.Minha equipe é esforçada, solidária e tranquila.
5Together, we are stronger than any of us alone.Juntos, somos mais fortes do que qualquer um de nós sozinho.
Aula 09 -- Pre-class

Asking Smart Questions in Meetings -- The Meeting Q&A

Question forms in every tense (What do you think? When did it start? Have you finished? Will it work?) plus polite, indirect questions (Could you tell me...). New vocabulary: clarify, follow up, elaborate, bring up, point out, concern, assumption, rephrase, input, feedback.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Clarify -- to make something clearer by explaining or asking
"Could you clarify when the simulations start?" (Esclarecer)
Follow up -- to ask a further question based on an answer
"I would like to follow up on your last point." (Dar seguimento)
Elaborate -- to explain something in more detail
"Can you elaborate on the new schedule?" (Detalhar)
Bring up -- to mention or introduce a topic in a meeting
"I want to bring up a safety concern." (Mencionar)
Point out -- to make someone notice something important
"Let me point out a problem with the timeline." (Destacar)
Concern -- a worry you want to ask or talk about
"My main concern is patient safety." (Preocupação)
Assumption -- something you believe is true without checking it
"We should not make that assumption without data." (Suposição)
Rephrase -- to say something again in different words
"Could you rephrase the question, please?" (Reformular)
Input -- opinions and ideas that people contribute
"I would like your input on the budget." (Contribuição)
Feedback -- comments about how good or useful something is
"Can I get your feedback on the plan?" (Retorno)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Clarify
Follow up
Elaborate
Bring up
Point out
Concern
Assumption
Rephrase
Input
Feedback

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Andreia is in a planning meeting. Before she speaks, she thinks about her questions. "What do we need before the first workshop?" she asks David. Then she wants to clarify the dates: "When did the supplier confirm the new console?" David answers, and Andreia decides to follow up: "Have we trained enough nurses for the night shift?" She also wants to bring up a concern: "What will we do if the equipment fails during a session?" David asks her to elaborate, so she explains her idea. At the end, he asks for her feedback, and he thanks her for her input.

1. Which helper verb does Andreia use for her past simple question?
A "did" (When did the supplier confirm...)
B "will" (What will we do...)
C "have" (Have we trained...)
2. What does Andreia want to bring up in the meeting?
A A concern about what happens if the equipment fails.
B A complaint about her salary.
C A request to leave early.
3. Why does David ask Andreia to elaborate?
A Because he wants her to explain her idea in more detail.
B Because he wants her to stop talking.
C Because he did not hear the question.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Question Forms

GRAMMAR

Usado para perguntar em qualquer tempo verbal. / Used to ask questions in any tense.

Tense / TempoHelper / AuxiliarExample / Exemplo
Present simpledo / doesWhat do you think?
Past simpledidWhen did it start?
Present continuousam / is / areWhat are you testing?
Present perfecthave / hasHave you finished?
FuturewillWill it work?

Key point / Ponto-chave: a ordem é sempre QUESTION WORD + HELPER + SUBJECT + MAIN VERB. O auxiliar carrega o tempo, então o verbo principal fica na forma base: diga "When did it start?", nunca "When did it started?". E nunca tire o auxiliar: "What do you think?", não "What you think?".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Could you when the simulations start?"
"I want to a concern about patient safety."
"What you think about the plan?"
"When the training start?"
" you tested the new console?"
"Can I get your on the plan?"

Stage 2: Order Andreia's Questions

Order

Listen first, then put Andreia's meeting questions in the order she asked them.

?Next, she brought up a concern about patient safety.
?First, she asked when the simulations would start.
?Finally, she asked for feedback on the whole plan.
?Then she asked how many nurses they could train each month.
?After that, she pointed out a risk with the small operating room.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Could you clarify when the simulations start?
Você poderia esclarecer quando começam as simulações?
I want to bring up a concern about patient safety.
Eu quero mencionar uma preocupação sobre a segurança do paciente.
Have you tested the new console with the team?
Você testou o novo console com a equipe?
What will we do if the equipment fails?
O que faremos se o equipamento falhar?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best question for each situation.

You want to ask about the coordinator's opinion (present simple). Which is correct?
A "What do you think about the plan?"
B "What you think about the plan?"
C "What thinks you about the plan?"
You want to ask when something happened (past simple). Which is correct?
A "When did the training started?"
B "When did the training start?"
C "When the training did start?"
You want to ask politely for clarification. Which is best?
A "Could you tell me when the meeting starts?"
B "Could you tell me when does the meeting start?"
C "Tell me when starts the meeting?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine your next real meeting at the hospital. In one minute, ask five smart questions in different tenses (What do...? When did...? Have you...? Will we...?). Bring up one concern, point out one risk, and ask for feedback or input from your team.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1Could you clarify when the simulations start?Você poderia esclarecer quando começam as simulações?
2Can I bring up a concern about patient safety?Posso mencionar uma preocupação sobre a segurança do paciente?
3Have we tested the new console with the whole team?Nós testamos o novo console com toda a equipe?
4What will we do if the equipment fails during a session?O que faremos se o equipamento falhar durante uma sessão?
5Could I get your feedback on the whole plan?Eu poderia ter o seu retorno sobre todo o plano?
Aula 10 -- Pre-class

Block 1 Review -- My Professional Identity

All tenses together in one self-presentation: present simple (who you are), past simple (how you started), present perfect (what you have accomplished), present continuous (what you are doing now), and the future (where you are going). New vocabulary: introduce, overview, expertise, strength, highlight, accomplish, role, career path, aspiration, summarize.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Introduce -- to present yourself or someone to others
"Let me introduce myself to the team." (Apresentar)
Overview -- a short, general description of something
"Let me give you a quick overview of my career." (Visão geral)
Expertise -- special skill or knowledge in a field
"My expertise is the da Vinci robotic system." (Especialidade)
Strength -- something you are good at; a strong point
"My main strength is staying calm under pressure." (Ponto forte)
Highlight -- to draw attention to something important
"Let me highlight one important strength." (Ressaltar)
Accomplish -- to finish something successfully; to achieve
"I have accomplished a lot in the operating room." (Realizar)
Role -- your job or function in a team or company
"My role today is in robotic surgery." (Função)
Career path -- the series of steps in your professional life
"My career path started in the intensive care unit." (Trajetória)
Aspiration -- a strong hope or goal for the future
"My aspiration is to lead the training program." (Aspiração)
Summarize -- to give the main points in a few words
"To summarize, I am ready for the next step." (Resumir)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Introduce
Overview
Expertise
Strength
Highlight
Accomplish
Role
Career path
Aspiration
Summarize

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Andreia stands up to introduce herself at the program kickoff. She gives a short overview of her career path. "I am a nurse, and my role is in robotic surgery," she says (present simple). Then she goes back in time: "I started in the intensive care unit" (past simple). She wants to highlight her experience: "Since then, I have trained six nurses" (present perfect). She also describes the present: "Right now, I am studying for a certification" (present continuous). Finally, she shares her aspiration: "Next year, I will lead the program" (future). To summarize, she presents her whole professional identity, using every tense.

1. Which tense does Andreia use to talk about her job today?
A Present simple ("I am a nurse").
B Past simple ("I started").
C Future ("I will lead").
2. Which tense does she use for her experience up to now?
A Present continuous ("I am studying").
B Present perfect ("I have trained six nurses").
C Past simple ("I started").
3. What does Andreia do at the end of her presentation?
A She summarizes her professional identity in a few words.
B She asks for a raise.
C She leaves without a conclusion.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- All Tenses Together

GRAMMAR

Usado para escolher o tempo verbal de cada parte da sua apresentação. / Used to choose the tense for each part of your presentation.

Part / ParteTense / TempoExample / Exemplo
Who you are todayPresent simpleI work in robotic surgery.
How you startedPast simpleI started in the ICU.
Experience up to nowPresent perfectI have trained six nurses.
What is happening nowPresent continuousI am studying now.
Where you are goingFuture (will / going to)I will lead the program.

Key point / Ponto-chave: escolha o tempo pelo TEMPO de cada parte. Use o present perfect para algo que começou no passado e continua agora: diga "I have worked here for six years", nunca "I work here for six years". Para uma ação terminada com palavra de tempo passado (last year, in 2010), use o past simple.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Let me myself to the team."
"My is robotic surgery."
"I my career in the ICU."
"I trained six new nurses."
"My is to lead the program."
"To , I am ready for the next step."

Stage 2: Order Andreia's Presentation

Order

Listen first, then put the parts of Andreia's self-presentation in the right order.

?Next, she highlighted what she has accomplished so far.
?First, she introduced herself and explained her role today.
?Finally, she shared her aspiration for the future.
?Then she told the story of how her career started.
?After that, she described what she is working on right now.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Let me introduce myself and give you a quick overview.
Deixe-me me apresentar e dar uma visão geral rápida.
I have accomplished a lot in the operating room.
Eu realizei muito no centro cirúrgico.
Right now, I am studying for a new certification.
Agora mesmo, estou estudando para uma nova certificação.
To summarize, my aspiration is to lead the program.
Resumindo, minha aspiração é liderar o programa.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You started at the hospital in 2010 and you still work there. Which is correct?
A "I have worked here since 2010."
B "I work here since 2010."
C "I working here since 2010."
You want to talk about a finished action last year. Which is correct?
A "Last year I have started a course."
B "Last year I started a course."
C "Last year I start a course."
You want to introduce yourself at a kickoff. Which is best?
A "Let me introduce myself and give a quick overview of my role."
B "Let me to introduce myself and give a quick overview."
C "Let me introducing myself and give a quick overview."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are at the program kickoff. In about two minutes, give your full self-presentation: introduce yourself (present simple), say how your career started (past simple), what you have accomplished (present perfect), what you are doing now (present continuous), and your aspiration (future). Then summarize your professional identity.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1Let me introduce myself and give you a quick overview.Deixe-me me apresentar e dar uma visão geral rápida.
2My role is in robotic surgery, and my expertise is the da Vinci system.Minha função é em cirurgia robótica, e minha especialidade é o sistema da Vinci.
3Since then, I have accomplished a lot in the operating room.Desde então, eu realizei muito no centro cirúrgico.
4Right now, I am leading a small team and studying for a certification.Agora mesmo, estou liderando uma pequena equipe e estudando para uma certificação.
5To summarize, my aspiration is to lead the program next year.Resumindo, minha aspiração é liderar o programa no próximo ano.
Aula 11 -- Pre-class

Opening a Meeting -- Agendas & Roles

Open and run a meeting in the present simple: state the objective, go through the agenda, assign the roles, then recap and wrap up. New vocabulary: agenda, chairperson, attendee, minutes, item, objective, recap, cover, wrap up, move on.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Agenda -- the list of topics to discuss in a meeting
"Let me go through the agenda for today." (Pauta)
Chairperson -- the person who leads and runs the meeting
"As the chairperson, I open the meeting." (Moderador)
Attendee -- a person who takes part in a meeting
"We have four attendees in the room today." (Participante)
Minutes -- the written record of what a meeting decides
"Lewis takes the minutes of every meeting." (Ata)
Item -- a single topic or point on the agenda
"The first item is the training schedule." (Ponto da pauta)
Objective -- the goal you want to reach in the meeting
"Our objective is to plan the training." (Objetivo)
Recap -- a short review of the main points
"At the end, I recap the main decisions." (Recapitular)
Cover -- to deal with or discuss a topic
"We cover three items in this meeting." (Abordar)
Wrap up -- to finish or conclude something
"Let's wrap up and finish on time." (Encerrar)
Move on -- to go to the next topic or item
"Let's move on to the next item." (Seguir adiante)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Agenda
Chairperson
Attendee
Minutes
Item
Objective
Recap
Cover
Wrap up
Move on

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Every Monday, Andreia chairs the weekly robotics meeting. The meeting starts at nine o'clock and ends at ten. There are four attendees. First, she welcomes everyone and states the objective: "Our objective is to plan the training." Then she goes through the agenda. There are three items, and each item takes fifteen minutes. Lewis takes the minutes, so the team keeps a clear record. They cover the schedule first, then they move on to the equipment. At the end, Andreia recaps the main points and they wrap up on time. A good chairperson always keeps the meeting on track.

1. Why do we say "the meeting starts" and not "the meeting start"?
A Because "the meeting" is it, and he/she/it takes verb + s.
B Because it is in the past.
C Because it is a question.
2. What does Andreia do right after she welcomes everyone?
A She wraps up the meeting.
B She states the objective and goes through the agenda.
C She leaves the room.
3. Who takes the minutes?
A Andreia takes the minutes.
B Lewis takes the minutes.
C Nobody takes the minutes.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Simple for Facts & Roles

GRAMMAR

Usado para fatos, horários fixos e papéis em uma reunião. / Used for facts, fixed schedules and roles in a meeting.

Subject / SujeitoVerb / VerboExample / Exemplo
I / You / We / Theybase formWe cover three items.
He / She / Itverb + sLewis takes the minutes.
The meeting (it)verb + sThe meeting starts at nine.

Key point / Ponto-chave: com he, she, it (e "the meeting" = it) acrescente -s ao verbo: the meeting starts, Lewis takes, she chairs. Para abrir e conduzir use frases fixas (meeting frames): "Thank you all for coming.", "Our objective is...", "Let me go through the agenda.", "Let's move on to the next item.", "Let's wrap up."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Let me go through the for today."
"Our is to plan the training."
"The meeting at nine o'clock."
"Lewis the minutes today."
"Let's to the next item."
"At the end, I the main points."

Stage 2: Order the Meeting Opening

Order

Listen first, then put the steps Andreia uses to open the meeting in the right order.

?Next, she goes through the agenda and presents the three items.
?First, she welcomes everyone and thanks them for coming.
?Finally, she starts with the first item and the meeting begins.
?Then she states the objective of the meeting.
?After that, she asks one attendee to take the minutes.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Thank you all for coming. Let's get started.
Obrigada a todos por virem. Vamos começar.
Let me go through the agenda. We have three items.
Deixe-me passar pela pauta. Temos três pontos.
The meeting starts at nine o'clock.
A reunião começa às nove horas.
Let me recap the main points, and then we wrap up.
Deixe-me recapitular os pontos principais, e então encerramos.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to open the meeting and welcome everyone. Which is best?
A "Thank you all for coming. Let's get started."
B "Why are you all here?"
C "The meeting is finished."
You talk about a fixed schedule. Which is correct?
A "The meeting start at nine."
B "The meeting starts at nine."
C "The meeting starting at nine."
You finished one item and want to go to the next. Which is best?
A "Let's move on to the next item."
B "Let's stop the meeting now."
C "We move on the next item to."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you chair your weekly meeting. In about two minutes, open the meeting: welcome the attendees, state the objective, go through the agenda with three items, ask someone to take the minutes, and explain how long each item takes. Then say how you will recap and wrap up. Use the present simple.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1Thank you all for coming. Let's get started.Obrigada a todos por virem. Vamos começar.
2Our objective today is to plan the training.Nosso objetivo hoje é planejar o treinamento.
3Let me go through the agenda. We have three items.Deixe-me passar pela pauta. Temos três pontos.
4Let's move on to the next item.Vamos seguir adiante para o próximo ponto.
5Let me recap the main points, and then we wrap up.Deixe-me recapitular os pontos principais, e então encerramos.
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

Talking Numbers -- Data, Volumes & Results

Present surgical data with confidence: report figures, volumes and rates, use quantifiers, and describe a trend. New vocabulary: figure, volume, rate, average, increase, decrease, roughly, target, trend, amount.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Figure -- a number that shows an amount or a total
"The total figure is roughly four hundred procedures." (Cifra)
Volume -- the total quantity of something over a period
"Our monthly volume increases steadily." (Volume)
Rate -- a measure shown as a number or percentage
"The complication rate is very low." (Taxa)
Average -- the typical or middle number in a set
"On average, we perform forty cases a week." (Média)
Increase -- a rise; to go up in number or amount
"We expect an increase of seven percent." (Aumento)
Decrease -- a fall; to go down in number or amount
"There is a small decrease in delays." (Queda)
Roughly -- approximately, not an exact number
"We perform roughly forty surgeries a month." (Aproximadamente)
Target -- the number you aim to reach
"Our target for next quarter is four hundred and fifty." (Meta)
Trend -- the general direction over time
"The trend is clearly positive." (Tendência)
Amount -- how much there is of something
"The amount of work is large this month." (Quantidade)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Figure
Volume
Rate
Average
Increase
Decrease
Roughly
Target
Trend
Amount

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Every quarter, Andreia presents the surgical data to the board. Last quarter, the team performed roughly four hundred and twenty robotic procedures. The total figure is strong. The monthly volume increases steadily: on average, the team performs about one hundred and forty surgeries a month. The complication rate stays very low, around two percent, so the trend is positive. There are plenty of cases and only a few delays. The amount of work is large, but the team is reliable. The target for next quarter is four hundred and fifty procedures, an increase of seven percent. Andreia always rounds the numbers and keeps the summary simple.

1. Why do we say "the volume increases" and not "the volume increase"?
A Because "the volume" is it, and he/she/it takes verb + s.
B Because it is in the past.
C Because it is a question.
2. How many surgeries does the team perform on average each month?
A Four hundred and fifty surgeries.
B About one hundred and forty surgeries.
C Roughly two surgeries.
3. We say "plenty of cases" but "a few delays". Why?
A "Plenty of" means a large amount; "a few" means a small number.
B They mean exactly the same thing.
C "A few" means zero.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Quantifiers & Data Language

GRAMMAR

Como falar de números e quantidades em uma apresentação. / How to talk about numbers and quantities in a presentation.

Quantifier / QuantificadorUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
many / a few + countablecontáveis: cases, proceduresWe have a few delays.
much / a little + uncountableincontáveis: work, dataWe do not have much data yet.
a lot of / plenty of + bothgrande quantidade (ambos)We have plenty of cases.

Data language / Linguagem de dados: use frases fixas para apresentar números: "The total figure is...", "On average, we perform...", "The volume increases by seven percent.", "The rate stays around two percent.", "Roughly four hundred procedures." Lembre do -s com he/she/it: the volume increases, the rate stays, the trend shows.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The total is roughly four hundred procedures."
"On , we perform one hundred and forty surgeries a month."
"The monthly volume steadily."
"The complication is around two percent."
"Our for next quarter is four hundred and fifty."
"We have plenty of cases and only a delays."

Stage 2: Order the Data Presentation

Order

Listen first, then put the steps Andreia uses to present the data in the right order.

?Next, she reports the complication rate as a percentage.
?First, she gives the total figure for the quarter.
?Finally, she summarizes the numbers and answers questions.
?Then she explains the monthly volume and the trend.
?After that, she compares the result with the target.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

The total figure is roughly four hundred procedures.
A cifra total é de aproximadamente quatrocentos procedimentos.
On average, we perform one hundred and forty surgeries a month.
Em média, realizamos cento e quarenta cirurgias por mês.
The volume increases by seven percent.
O volume aumenta em sete por cento.
The trend is positive, and we are on track to reach our target.
A tendência é positiva, e estamos no caminho para atingir nossa meta.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to give an approximate total. Which is best?
A "We perform roughly four hundred procedures a quarter."
B "We perform exactly maybe four hundred procedures."
C "We perform a procedure of four hundred."
You talk about a small number of countable delays. Which is correct?
A "We have much delays."
B "We have a few delays."
C "We have a little delays."
You describe a number going up over time. Which is best?
A "The volume increases by ten percent."
B "The volume increase ten percent of."
C "The volume decrease up ten percent."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you present your team's results to the board. In about two minutes, give the total figure for the quarter, explain the monthly volume and the trend, report a rate as a percentage, and compare the result with your target. Use quantifiers (plenty of, a few, on average) and remember the -s with he, she, it.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1The total figure is roughly four hundred procedures.A cifra total é de aproximadamente quatrocentos procedimentos.
2On average, we perform one hundred and forty surgeries a month.Em média, realizamos cento e quarenta cirurgias por mês.
3The volume increases by seven percent.O volume aumenta em sete por cento.
4The complication rate stays around two percent.A taxa de complicações fica em torno de dois por cento.
5The trend is positive, and we are on track to reach our target.A tendência é positiva, e estamos no caminho para atingir nossa meta.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Asking For & Giving Updates

Report progress and status: say what is complete, pending or behind, and ask a colleague for an update. New vocabulary: update, progress, status, on schedule, behind, ahead, pending, complete, milestone, on hold.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Catch up -- to recover lost time; reach the level you should be at
"We have a clear plan to catch up on the delays." (Recuperar o atraso)
Flag -- to point out a problem or concern so people notice it
"I want to flag two delays in the project." (Sinalizar)
Status -- the current state of a task or project
"What is the status of the simulator setup?" (Situação)
On schedule -- on time; happening as planned
"The manual is on schedule." (No prazo)
Behind -- late; slower than the plan
"We are slightly behind on two tasks." (Atrasado)
Ahead -- earlier or faster than the plan
"We are ahead of plan on the training." (Adiantado)
Pending -- still waiting to be done or decided
"The simulator setup is still pending." (Pendente)
Complete -- fully finished; nothing left to do
"The training manual is complete." (Concluído)
Milestone -- an important step or point reached in a project
"We have reached our first milestone on time." (Marco)
On hold -- paused or stopped for now
"That task is on hold until the console arrives." (Em espera)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Catch up
Flag
Status
On schedule
Behind
Ahead
Pending
Complete
Milestone
On hold

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before the meeting, Andreia prepares a quick update on the training project. Overall, the project is on schedule. The training manual is complete -- David has just approved it, and Andreia has already shared it with the team. They have already reached their first milestone, the basic certification, on time. The nurse training is ahead of plan: she has already trained four of the six nurses. Two tasks are behind. The simulator setup is still pending, because the vendor has not delivered the second console yet, so the task is on hold. The final assessment has not started yet. Andreia keeps the status clear: what is done, what is waiting, and what is late.

1. Why do we say "David has just approved it" and not "David just approved it"?
A Because the present perfect (has + approved) links a recent action to now.
B Because it happened a long time ago.
C Because it is a question.
2. The console has not arrived yet. Where does "yet" go?
