A Before the verb, like "already".
B At the end of the sentence, in negatives and questions.
C At the start of the sentence.
3. Which task is on hold, and why?
A The simulator setup, because the vendor has not delivered the console yet.
B The training manual, because it is complete.
C The nurse training, because it is ahead of plan.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect with just, already & yet

GRAMMAR

Como dar um status usando o present perfect. / How to give a status using the present perfect.

Word / PalavraUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
justterminou agora há pouco (antes do verbo)I have just approved it.
alreadyfeito antes do esperado (antes do verbo)I have already shared it.
yetainda não; em negativas e perguntas (no fim)It has not arrived yet. / Have you finished yet?

Estrutura / Structure: have/has + past participle (particípio). Afirmativa: "I have already finished." Negativa: "It has not arrived yet." Pergunta: "Have you finished yet?" Atenção: yet NUNCA aparece em frases afirmativas -- nelas usamos just ou already.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I have sent the report -- two minutes ago."
"We have reached the first milestone."
"The vendor has not delivered the console ."
"The simulator setup is still ."
"Overall, the project is on ."
"Have you finished the assessment ?"

Stage 2: Order the Status Update

Order

Listen first, then put the steps Andreia uses to give her update in the right order.

?Next, she explains what she has already finished.
?First, she says the project is on schedule overall.
?Finally, she gives a clear plan to catch up and invites questions.
?Then she reports the good news: the manual is complete and the first milestone is done.
?After that, she reports the two delays and what is still pending.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Here is a quick update on the project.
Aqui está uma atualização rápida do projeto.
The manual is complete. I have just finished it.
O manual está concluído. Acabei de terminá-lo.
That task is still pending. It has not started yet.
Essa tarefa ainda está pendente. Ela não começou ainda.
Have you finished the report yet?
Você já terminou o relatório?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You finished a task one minute ago. How do you report it?
A "I have just finished the task."
B "I have yet finished the task."
C "I finish just the task."
A task is not done. How do you say it is still waiting?
A "The task did not finish yet."
B "The task has not finished yet."
C "The task has yet not finished."
You want to ask a colleague about their progress. Which is best?
A "Have you sent the email yet?"
B "Have you yet sent the email?"
C "Did you send the email already yet?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you give a status update on one of your real projects. In about two minutes, say what is on schedule, what is complete, what you have already done, what is still pending or on hold, and ask one yet-question to a colleague. Use the present perfect with just, already and yet.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1Here is a quick update on the project.Aqui está uma atualização rápida do projeto.
2The manual is complete. I have just finished it.O manual está concluído. Acabei de terminá-lo.
3We have already reached the first milestone.Nós já atingimos o primeiro marco.
4That task is still pending. It has not started yet.Essa tarefa ainda está pendente. Ela não começou ainda.
5Have you finished the report yet?Você já terminou o relatório?
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

Solving Problems at Work

Describe a problem and suggest a solution: name the issue, the root cause and the impact, then propose a fix. New vocabulary: issue, root cause, bottleneck, downtime, troubleshoot, workaround, escalate, resolve, propose, fix.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Issue -- a problem or matter that needs attention
"Let me explain the issue with the simulator." (Problema / questão)
Root cause -- the real, underlying reason for a problem
"The root cause is a missed shipping date." (Causa raiz)
Bottleneck -- one slow point that blocks the whole process
"The missing console is the bottleneck." (Gargalo)
Downtime -- a period when work or a system stops
"We have downtime in the lab every morning." (Tempo parado / inatividade)
Troubleshoot -- to find the cause of a problem and fix it
"Let's troubleshoot the setup together." (Diagnosticar e resolver)
Workaround -- a temporary solution that gets around a problem
"A simple workaround keeps us on schedule." (Solução provisória / contorno)
Escalate -- to take a problem to a higher level or manager
"If it is serious, we should escalate it to the director." (Escalar / levar a uma instância superior)
Resolve -- to solve a problem fully and end it
"I need your help to resolve this before the meeting." (Resolver / solucionar)
Propose -- to suggest a plan or idea for others to consider
"I want to propose one clear solution." (Propor)
Fix -- to repair something; also, the solution itself
"We have a quick fix for the lab." (Conserto / solução)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Issue
Root cause
Bottleneck
Downtime
Troubleshoot
Workaround
Escalate
Resolve
Propose
Fix

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before the meeting, Andreia describes a problem and suggests a solution. First, she names the issue: the second console has not arrived. That is the main bottleneck. The root cause is simple -- the vendor missed the shipping date. The impact is real: the lab has downtime, and two nurses cannot practice. Then she suggests three ideas. "We could borrow a console from the cardiology team," she says. "Or we should ask the vendor for a firm new date. And why don't we move the theory classes forward?" She proposes one clear workaround, and she adds: "What if the vendor misses the date again? Then we should escalate the issue to the director." With a calm plan, the team can resolve the problem and stay on schedule.

1. Andreia says "We could borrow a console." What does could express here?
A A possible option or suggestion -- one idea among several.
B Something that already happened in the past.
C A strict rule that must be followed.
2. After "Why don't we", which form of the verb do we use?
A The -ing form: "Why don't we moving the classes?"
B The base verb: "Why don't we move the classes?"
C The past form: "Why don't we moved the classes?"
3. When does Andreia say the team should escalate the problem?
A If the vendor misses the new date again -- when the issue becomes serious.
B Immediately, before trying any workaround.
C Never, because the problem is not important.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Making Suggestions (should, could, why don't we, what if)

GRAMMAR

Como sugerir uma solução de forma educada. / How to suggest a solution politely.

Structure / EstruturaUse / UsoExample / Exemplo
should + base verbconselho forte; a melhor ideiaWe should ask the vendor for a new date.
could + base verbuma opção possível (mais suave)We could borrow a console for one week.
Why don't we + base verb?sugestão amigável para o grupoWhy don't we move the classes forward?
What if + subject + verb?testar um cenário ou ideiaWhat if the vendor misses the date again?

Atenção / Note: depois de should, could e why don't we, use sempre o verbo na forma base, sem "to": "We should ask" (não "to ask"), "Why don't we move" (não "moving"). Use should para a sua melhor recomendação, e could para uma opção mais aberta. / After these forms, always use the base verb without "to".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Let me explain the with the simulator."
"The is a missed shipping date."
"We borrow a console for one week."
" move the theory classes forward?"
"If it is serious, we should it to the director."
"This simple keeps us on schedule."

Stage 2: Order the Problem Brief

Order

Listen first, then put the steps Andreia uses to present the problem and her solution in the right order.

?Next, she describes the impact: downtime in the lab.
?First, she names the problem clearly: the simulator setup.
?Finally, she says when to escalate the issue to the director.
?Then she explains the root cause: a missed shipping date.
?After that, she suggests two solutions, using should and why don't we.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Let me explain the issue and the root cause.
Deixe-me explicar o problema e a causa raiz.
We could borrow a console for one week.
Poderíamos pegar um console emprestado por uma semana.
Why don't we move the classes forward?
Por que não adiantamos as aulas?
If it is serious, we should escalate it.
Se for sério, devemos escalá-lo.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to suggest a possible option, without pushing it. Which is best?
A "We could ask the cardiology team for help."
B "We could to ask the cardiology team for help."
C "We could asking the cardiology team for help."
You want to make a friendly suggestion to the group. Which is best?
A "Why we don't move the meeting?"
B "Why don't we move the meeting?"
C "Why don't we moving the meeting?"
You want to test an idea about a future risk. Which is best?
A "What if the console does not arrive on time?"
B "What if the console will not arrive on time?"
C "What the console does not arrive on time?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of a real problem at your work. In about two minutes, name the issue, explain the root cause and the impact, and then suggest a solution. Use should, could, why don't we and what if to make at least three suggestions, and say when you would escalate the problem.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1Let me explain the issue and the root cause.Deixe-me explicar o problema e a causa raiz.
2The impact is real: we have downtime in the lab.O impacto é real: temos tempo parado no laboratório.
3We could borrow a console for one week.Poderíamos pegar um console emprestado por uma semana.
4Why don't we move the classes forward?Por que não adiantamos as aulas?
5If it is serious, we should escalate it.Se for sério, devemos escalá-lo.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Reaching Agreement

Agree and disagree politely, reach a compromise, and turn the discussion into clear action items with owners and deadlines. New vocabulary: agree, disagree, point, compromise, consensus, action item, vote, assign, follow-up, finalize.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Agree -- to have or accept the same opinion as someone
"I think we all agree on the workaround." (Concordar)
Disagree -- to have a different opinion from someone
"I'm afraid I disagree about the timeline." (Discordar)
Point -- an idea or opinion you make in a discussion
"That is a fair point about the deadline." (Argumento / observação)
Compromise -- a middle solution where each side gives a little
"As a compromise, we add a buffer." (Meio-termo / acordo)
Consensus -- general agreement among the whole group
"Borrowing a console is the consensus." (Consenso)
Action item -- a specific task someone must do after a meeting
"Each action item needs one owner." (Tarefa / ação a executar)
Vote -- to formally choose a decision as a group, often by raising hands
"Let us vote on the final plan." (Votar)
Assign -- to give a task or responsibility to a person
"Let us assign one owner to each task." (Atribuir / designar)
Follow-up -- a later check to confirm a task was done
"Each task needs a follow-up date." (Acompanhamento)
Finalize -- to complete and agree on the final version of a plan
"We finalize the plan in the meeting." (Finalizar / fechar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Agree
Disagree
Point
Compromise
Consensus
Action item
Vote
Assign
Follow-up
Finalize

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

In the meeting, Andreia helps the team reach a decision. On the workaround, she says, "I agree. Borrowing a console is the right move. That is our consensus." On the timeline, opinions differ. A colleague wants a full month. Andreia does not say "no" directly. Instead she says, "I see your point, but a full month is too long." Then she offers a compromise: "Why don't we keep the date and add a three-day buffer?" The team accepts. Now they assign the action items. "I will set the vendor deadline by Friday," says Andreia. "David, can you follow up with cardiology by Thursday?" Each task has one owner and a follow-up date. With everyone on board, they finalize the plan today.

1. Andreia says "I see your point, but a full month is too long." What is she doing?
A Disagreeing politely -- she recognizes the other person first, then differs.
B Agreeing completely with the colleague.
C Refusing to discuss the timeline at all.
2. When Andreia assigns an action item, what does she always include?
A Only the task, with no owner and no date.
B One owner for the task and a clear deadline.
C A general promise to do it "soon".
3. What is the compromise the team accepts on the timeline?
A Keep the original date but add a three-day buffer.
B Delay the whole project by a full month.
C Cancel the practice sessions completely.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Agreeing, Disagreeing & Assigning

GRAMMAR

Como concordar, discordar com educação e atribuir tarefas. / How to agree, disagree politely and assign action items.

Function / FunçãoPhrase / FraseExample / Exemplo
ConcordarI agree / Good pointI agree, that is our consensus.
Discordar com educaçãoI see your point, but...I see your point, but the date is tight.
Meio-termoAs a compromise...As a compromise, we add a buffer.
Atribuir tarefaCan you / Would you + verbo base?Can you follow up by Friday?

Atenção / Note: "agree" é verbo -- diga I agree (não "I am agree"). Para discordar com educação, reconheça o outro primeiro ("I see your point") e depois use but. Ao atribuir uma tarefa, use Can you ou Would you + verbo base, e sempre nomeie o responsável e o prazo. / "agree" is a verb; recognize the other person before disagreeing; name the owner and the deadline.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I , that is our consensus."
"I see your , but the date is tight."
"As a , we add a three-day buffer."
"Let us one owner to each task."
"Let us on the final plan."
"We the plan today, with a follow-up on Monday."

Stage 2: Order the Decision

Order

Listen first, then put the steps Andreia uses to turn the discussion into a decision in the right order.

?Next, she handles the disagreement on the timeline and offers a compromise.
?First, she sums up where the team agrees: the workaround.
?Finally, she sets a follow-up date and finalizes the plan.
?Then she names the consensus clearly.
?After that, she assigns the action items, with one owner for each task.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I agree, that is our consensus.
Eu concordo, esse é o nosso consenso.
I see your point, but the date is tight.
Entendo seu ponto, mas a data está apertada.
As a compromise, we add a buffer.
Como meio-termo, adicionamos uma folga.
Can you follow up by Thursday?
Você pode acompanhar até quinta-feira?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to agree with a colleague. Which is best?
A "I agree with you on the workaround."
B "I am agree with you on the workaround."
C "I am agreeing you on the workaround."
You disagree about the timeline but want to stay polite. Which is best?
A "No, you are wrong about the timeline."
B "I see your point, but I'm not sure a month works."
C "That timeline is a bad idea."
You want to assign a follow-up to David with a deadline. Which is best?
A "David, can you confirm the loan by Thursday?"
B "David, can you to confirm the loan by Thursday?"
C "David, somebody should confirm the loan sometime."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of a real decision your team needs to make at work. In about two minutes, say where you agree, disagree politely on one point with "I see your point, but...", offer a compromise, and then assign two action items, each with an owner and a deadline. Finish by setting a follow-up date.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1I agree, that is our consensus.Eu concordo, esse é o nosso consenso.
2I see your point, but the date is tight.Entendo seu ponto, mas a data está apertada.
3As a compromise, we add a buffer.Como meio-termo, adicionamos uma folga.
4Can you follow up by Thursday?Você pode acompanhar até quinta-feira?
5Let us finalize the plan today.Vamos finalizar o plano hoje.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Making Requests & Proposals

Ask for what you need politely and propose ideas the professional way -- Could I, Could you, Would you mind, I'd like to propose -- then put a proposal in writing. New vocabulary: request, proposal, favor, willing, approval, budget, resource, justify, prioritize, draft.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Request -- a polite act of asking for something
"I have a small request for the committee." (Pedido / solicitação)
Proposal -- a plan or idea you formally suggest, often in writing
"I have prepared a one-page proposal." (Proposta)
Favor -- a small kind thing you ask someone to do for you
"I need a small favor from you." (Favor)
Approval -- official permission to go ahead with something
"I am asking for your approval to move forward." (Aprovação)
Budget -- the amount of money available for something
"I know the budget is tight." (Orçamento)
Resource -- something useful you need: people, time or equipment
"The console frees up a critical resource." (Recurso)
Justify -- to give good reasons that show something is right or worth it
"Let me justify the cost of the console." (Justificar)
Prioritize -- to treat something as more important and deal with it first
"I would like to prioritize patient safety." (Priorizar)
Willing -- happy and ready to do something
"I am willing to lead the project." (Disposto / disposta)
Draft -- a first version of a document, before the final one
"I will send you a draft by Wednesday." (Rascunho / minuta)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Request
Proposal
Favor
Approval
Budget
Resource
Justify
Prioritize
Willing
Draft

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Andreia wants a second training console, so she prepares a proposal. First she opens with a polite request: "Could I ask the committee to consider a second console?" Note: Could is softer than Can, so it sounds more polite. Then she gives the reason -- the team shares one console -- and explains the cost. The budget is tight, so she must justify it: the console frees up a critical resource and saves time. Above all, she wants to prioritize patient safety. She also asks her colleague David for a small favor: "Would you mind reviewing the draft?" Note: after Would you mind, the verb takes the -ing form (reviewing). David is willing to help. Finally, Andreia asks for approval to move forward, with a clear deadline.

1. Andreia says "Could I ask the committee to consider a second console?" Why "Could", not "Can"?
A "Could" is softer and sounds more polite at work.
B "Could" means the request is in the past.
C There is no difference between "Could" and "Can".
2. After "Would you mind", what form of the verb do we use?
A The base verb: "Would you mind review it?"
B The -ing form: "Would you mind reviewing it?"
C The infinitive: "Would you mind to review it?"
3. Why does Andreia need to justify the cost?
A Because the budget is tight, so she gives good reasons for the cost.
B Because the console is free and needs no reason.
C Because the committee already approved the budget.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Polite Requests & Proposing

GRAMMAR

Como pedir com educação e propor uma ideia. / How to make polite requests and propose an idea.

Function / FunçãoForm / FormaExample / Exemplo
Pedir permissãoCould I + verbo baseCould I ask for a second console?
Pedir a alguémCould you + verbo baseCould you review the proposal?
Muito educadoWould you mind + verbo-INGWould you mind reviewing it?
Propor uma ideiaI would like to propose...I would like to propose a console.

Atenção / Note: Could é mais suave que Can -- soa mais educado. Depois de Could I / Could you, use o verbo base (Could you send it?). Depois de Would you mind, use o verbo no -ING (Would you mind sending it?). Para propor, use I would like to propose + ideia, ou Would it be possible to + verbo base. / "Could" is softer than "Can"; use the base verb after Could you; use the -ing form after Would you mind.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I have a small for the committee."
"I have prepared a one-page ."
"I know the is tight."
"Let me the cost of the console."
"I would like to patient safety."
"I am asking for your to move forward."

Stage 2: Order the Proposal

Order

Listen first, then put the steps Andreia uses to build her proposal in the right order.

?Next, she justifies the cost, because the budget is tight.
?First, she opens with a polite request: Could I ask the committee to consider a second console?
?Finally, she asks for approval with a clear deadline.
?Then she gives the reason: practice time is limited.
?After that, she prioritizes patient safety as the main benefit.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Could I ask you a small favor?
Eu poderia te pedir um pequeno favor?
Would you mind reviewing the draft?
Você se importaria de revisar o rascunho?
I would like to propose a second console.
Eu gostaria de propor um segundo console.
I am asking for your approval to move forward.
Estou pedindo sua aprovação para seguir em frente.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to ask a colleague to review your draft, very politely. Which is best?
A "Would you mind reviewing my draft?"
B "Would you mind to review my draft?"
C "You review my draft now."
You want to ask the committee for permission, politely. Which is best?
A "Give me a second console."
B "Could I ask you to consider a second console?"
C "Can I to ask for a second console?"
You want to formally propose an idea to the committee. Which is best?
A "I would like to propose a second training console."
B "I would like propose a second training console."
C "I am propose a second training console."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of one real thing you need at work -- a tool, more time, or budget. In about two minutes, open with a polite request ("Could I..." or "Could you..."), propose the idea ("I would like to propose..."), justify the cost because the budget is tight, say what you prioritize, and finish by asking for approval with a clear deadline.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1Could I ask you a small favor?Eu poderia te pedir um pequeno favor?
2Could you review the proposal?Você poderia revisar a proposta?
3Would you mind reviewing the draft?Você se importaria de revisar o rascunho?
4I would like to propose a second console.Eu gostaria de propor um segundo console.
5I am asking for your approval to move forward.Estou pedindo sua aprovação para seguir em frente.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Handling Problems & Complaints

Solve an issue on a call the professional way -- apologize, reassure, and offer a solution with the first conditional (If we ship today, it will arrive Monday). New vocabulary: complaint, apologize, inconvenience, refund, replacement, urgent, reassure, delay, backup, solution.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Complaint -- when someone says they are not happy about a problem
"A surgeon filed a complaint about the delay." (Reclamação)
Apologize -- to say you are sorry about a problem or mistake
"I apologize for the inconvenience." (Pedir desculpas)
Inconvenience -- a small trouble or difficulty caused to someone
"Sorry for the inconvenience this caused you." (Transtorno / incômodo)
Delay -- when something happens later than planned
"The console has a two-week delay." (Atraso)
Urgent -- so important that it needs action right now
"This is urgent for the training team." (Urgente)
Reassure -- to make someone feel calm and less worried
"Let me reassure you: we have a plan." (Tranquilizar)
Solution -- a way to fix a problem or end a difficulty
"Let me offer a clear solution." (Solução)
Backup -- a second plan or unit you use if the first one fails
"We have a backup unit on loan." (Reserva / plano B)
Refund -- money given back when something goes wrong
"If the part is faulty, we will ask for a refund." (Reembolso)
Replacement -- a new item that takes the place of a broken or missing one
"They will send a replacement unit." (Substituição)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Complaint
Apologize
Inconvenience
Delay
Urgent
Reassure
Solution
Backup
Refund
Replacement

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The new console is delayed by two weeks, and a surgeon has filed a complaint. The matter is urgent, so Andreia calls to handle it. First she apologizes: "I apologize for the delay and the inconvenience." Then she offers a solution using the first conditional: "If the part is missing, we will order a new one." Note: the if part uses the present simple (is), and the result uses will + base verb (will order). She also explains the backup: "If the date slips again, we will use a loan unit." If the part is faulty, the supplier will send a replacement or give a refund. Finally, she wants to reassure the team: "If anyone is worried, please come and talk to me."

1. Andreia says "If the part is missing, we will order a new one." Why "is", not "will be"?
A In the first conditional, the if part uses the present simple.
B Because the part is already missing in the past.
C Both "is" and "will be" are correct here.
2. In the result part of a first conditional, which form do we use?
A The present simple: "we order a new one."
B Will + base verb: "we will order a new one."
C The past simple: "we ordered a new one."
3. What does Andreia do first when she handles the complaint?
A She apologizes for the delay and the inconvenience.
B She blames the supplier and ends the call.
C She says nothing about the problem.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The First Conditional

GRAMMAR

Como falar de um resultado futuro real que depende de uma condição. / How to talk about a real future result that depends on a condition.

Parte / PartForma / FormExemplo / Example
Condição (if)If + presente simplesIf the part is missing,
Resultadowill + verbo basewe will order a new one.
Ordem invertidaresultado + if + presenteWe will ship it if they confirm.
Oferecer ajudacan / will + verbo baseI can look into it. I will fix it.

Atenção / Note: O first conditional fala de um futuro real e possível. A parte do if usa o presente simples (If it arrives...) e o resultado usa will + verbo base (we will start...). NUNCA use will na parte do if. Para oferecer ajuda, use can ou will + verbo base. / In the first conditional, the if part uses the present simple and the result uses will + base verb. Never use will in the if part.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"A surgeon filed a about the delay."
"I for the inconvenience."
"The console has a two-week ."
"Let me offer a clear ."
"We have a loan unit as a ."
"Let me you: we have a plan."

Stage 2: Order the Call

Order

Listen first, then put the steps Andreia uses to handle the complaint call in the right order.

?Next, she offers a solution with the first conditional: if the date slips again, we will use a backup unit.
?First, she apologizes for the delay and the inconvenience.
?Finally, she promises a clear new date and a weekly update.
?Then she explains the problem: the supplier had a missing part.
?After that, she reassures the team that no real surgery is affected.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I apologize for the delay and the inconvenience.
Peço desculpas pelo atraso e pelo transtorno.
If the part is missing, we will order a new one.
Se a peça estiver faltando, vamos pedir uma nova.
Let me reassure you: we have a backup plan.
Deixe-me tranquilizá-lo: temos um plano reserva.
If they confirm by Friday, we will keep the schedule.
Se eles confirmarem até sexta, vamos manter o cronograma.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

A colleague complains about a late delivery. How do you respond first?
A "I apologize for the delay and the inconvenience."
B "It is not my problem."
C "You should not complain."
You want to offer a future solution that depends on a condition. Which is correct?
A "If the part will be missing, we will order a new one."
B "If the part is missing, we will order a new one."
C "If the part is missing, we order will a new one."
A surgeon is worried about the delay. How do you reassure them?
A "Let me reassure you: no real surgery is affected."
B "I do not know what will happen."
C "Please stop worrying about it."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of one real problem at work -- a delay, a complaint, or a faulty item. In about two minutes, apologize for the inconvenience, explain the problem, offer a first-conditional solution ("If..., we will..."), name a backup plan, and reassure the other person with a clear new date.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1I apologize for the delay and the inconvenience.Peço desculpas pelo atraso e pelo transtorno.
2If the part is missing, we will order a new one.Se a peça estiver faltando, vamos pedir uma nova.
3Let me reassure you: we have a backup plan.Deixe-me tranquilizá-lo: temos um plano reserva.
4I can look into it, and I will fix it today.Posso verificar isso e vou resolver hoje.
5If they confirm by Friday, we will keep the schedule.Se eles confirmarem até sexta, vamos manter o cronograma.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Negotiating Solutions

Make offers and trade-offs the professional way and reach a fair deal with the second conditional (If you gave us thirty days, we would sign today). New vocabulary: negotiate, offer, concession, trade-off, term, flexible, counteroffer, deal, mutual, agreement.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Negotiate -- to talk with someone to reach an agreement both sides accept
"We negotiate the terms of the deal." (Negociar)
Offer -- something you propose to give or do in a deal
"Let me make you an offer." (Oferta)
Concession -- something you give up so the other side gains
"I am ready to make one concession on the date." (Concessão)
Trade-off -- when you give one thing in order to get another
"Let me offer a trade-off: a longer warranty for full payment." (Troca / contrapartida)
Term -- one of the conditions in a deal, such as price or date
"The terms are still flexible." (Termo / condição)
Flexible -- able to change your terms or plans easily
"Thank you for your flexibility; the terms are flexible." (Flexível)
Counteroffer -- a different offer you make in reply to theirs
"Listen for their counteroffer before you decide." (Contraproposta)
Deal -- an agreement where both sides accept the terms
"I am confident we can reach a deal today." (Acordo / negócio)
Mutual -- shared by both sides; good for everyone in the deal
"That feels like a mutual win." (Mútuo / recíproco)
Agreement -- the final deal that both sides accept and sign
"Shall we draft an agreement along these lines?" (Acordo / contrato)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Negotiate
Offer
Concession
Trade-off
Term
Flexible
Counteroffer
Deal
Mutual
Agreement

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Andreia must negotiate a fair deal for the replacement console. The supplier wants full payment now, but the terms are still flexible. First she makes an offer using the second conditional: "If you gave us thirty days, we would sign today." Note: the if part uses the past simple (gave), and the result uses would + base verb (would sign) -- the situation is imagined, a soft way to propose. Then she proposes a trade-off: "If we paid up front, we would ask for a longer warranty." She listens for the supplier's counteroffer. She is ready to make one concession on the date if they make one on price. The goal is a mutual win. If both sides agreed, they would draft an agreement everyone can live with.

1. Andreia says "If you gave us thirty days, we would sign today." Why "gave", not "give"?
A In the second conditional, the if part uses the past simple.
B Because it already happened in the past.
C Both "give" and "gave" are correct here.
2. In the result part of a second conditional, which form do we use?
A Will + base verb: "we will sign today."
B Would + base verb: "we would sign today."
C The past simple: "we signed today."
3. What is the goal of Andreia's negotiation?
A A mutual win -- a deal good for both sides, with a trade-off and a concession.
B To win everything and give nothing in return.
C To accept the supplier's first number without question.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Second Conditional

GRAMMAR

Como fazer uma oferta ou troca imaginada numa negociação. / How to make an imagined offer or trade-off in a negotiation.

Parte / PartForma / FormExemplo / Example
Condição (if)If + passado simplesIf you gave us thirty days,
Resultadowould + verbo basewe would sign today.
Ordem invertidaresultado + if + passadoWe would sign if you gave us time.
Usooferta / troca imaginadaIf we paid up front, we would ask for more.

Atenção / Note: O second conditional fala de uma situação imaginada ou improvável. A parte do if usa o passado simples (If you offered...) e o resultado usa would + verbo base (we would accept...). É um jeito educado e suave de fazer uma oferta. Compare: first conditional = real/provável (If they confirm, we will sign); second conditional = imaginado (If they gave us time, we would sign). / The second conditional uses the past simple in the if part and would + base verb in the result, for an imagined offer.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Let me make you an ."
"The are still flexible."
"Let me offer a ."
"I am ready to make one ."
"That feels like a win."
"Shall we draft an along these lines?"

Stage 2: Order the Deal

Order

Listen first, then put the steps Andreia uses to negotiate the deal in the right order.

?Next, she proposes a trade-off: if we paid up front, we would ask for a longer warranty.
?First, she states her position: the terms are flexible, but the training date and the budget matter most.
?Finally, she aims for a mutual agreement that works for both sides.
?Then she makes an offer with the second conditional: if you gave us thirty days, we would sign today.
?After that, she offers one concession and asks for one in return.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

If you gave us thirty days, we would sign today.
Se vocês nos dessem trinta dias, nós assinaríamos hoje.
Here is a trade-off: full payment for a longer warranty.
Aqui vai uma troca: pagamento integral por uma garantia maior.
I will make one concession if you make one in return.
Eu faço uma concessão se vocês fizerem uma em troca.
Shall we draft an agreement along these lines?
Podemos redigir um acordo nesses moldes?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to make a soft, imagined offer in a negotiation. Which is correct?
A "If you would give us thirty days, we would sign today."
B "If you gave us thirty days, we would sign today."
C "If you gave us thirty days, we will sign today."
The supplier insists on full payment. How do you propose a trade-off?
A "If we paid up front, we would ask for a longer warranty."
B "Just take the money, no conditions."
C "If we paid up front, we will asked for a warranty."
You want to close with a deal good for both sides. Which is best?
A "If we agreed on these terms, we would both gain. Shall we draft the agreement?"
B "Take it or leave it."
C "I want everything and give nothing."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of one real deal you might negotiate at work -- a price, a deadline, or a contract term. In about two minutes, state your position, make an offer with the second conditional ("If you..., we would..."), propose a trade-off, offer one concession and ask for one in return, and aim for a mutual agreement.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1If you gave us thirty days, we would sign today.Se vocês nos dessem trinta dias, nós assinaríamos hoje.
2Here is a trade-off: full payment for a longer warranty.Aqui vai uma troca: pagamento integral por uma garantia maior.
3I will make one concession if you make one in return.Eu faço uma concessão se vocês fizerem uma em troca.
4That feels like a mutual win for both sides.Isso parece uma vitória mútua para os dois lados.
5Shall we draft an agreement along these lines?Podemos redigir um acordo nesses moldes?
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Giving & Receiving Feedback

Praise strengths and suggest improvements the professional way, and soften difficult messages with past modals (You could have explained the risk earlier). New vocabulary: constructive, praise, strengths, improve, suggestion, acknowledge, perspective, address, framework, honest.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Constructive -- helpful and meant to make the work better, not to criticize harshly
"Let me give you some constructive feedback." (Construtivo)
Praise -- to say warmly what someone did well
"I want to praise the team for their hard work." (Elogiar)
Strengths -- the things a person or a team does well
"One of your strengths is staying calm under pressure." (Pontos fortes)
Improve -- to make something better than before
"Here is one way to improve the report." (Melhorar)
Suggestion -- a gentle idea you offer to help, not an order
"May I make a small suggestion?" (Sugestão)
Acknowledge -- to accept feedback and show that you heard it
"Thank you. I acknowledge your point and I will work on it." (Reconhecer)
Perspective -- the way one person sees or understands a situation
"From my perspective, the timing was the main issue." (Perspectiva)
Address -- to deal with a problem or topic directly
"Let us address the delay before the next project." (Tratar / abordar)
Framework -- a simple structure you follow, such as praise first, then a suggestion
"I use a simple framework: strengths first, then one improvement." (Estrutura / modelo)
Honest -- truthful and clear, but still respectful and kind
"Let me be honest and direct, but I say it kindly." (Honesto / sincero)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Constructive
Praise
Strengths
Improve
Suggestion
Acknowledge
Perspective
Address
Framework
Honest

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Andreia must give the team some constructive feedback after the console rollout. She uses a simple framework: strengths first, then one suggestion. She begins with praise: "Your communication was excellent." Then she gives the difficult part softly, with a past modal: "You could have explained the risk earlier." Note: the form is could have + past participle (explained), and it points to a better past choice politely. She adds her view: "From my perspective, we should have tested the console first." She wants to improve the process, not to blame anyone, so she is honest but kind. Finally, she will address any questions and acknowledge the team's effort.

1. Andreia says "You could have explained the risk earlier." What does this mean?
A In the past, explaining earlier was a possible, better choice -- but it did not happen.
B She is talking about something the team will do tomorrow.
C The team explained the risk early and she is happy.
2. Which form follows could / should / would in past modals?
A The base verb: "could have explain."
B have + past participle: "could have explained."
C of + past participle: "could of explained."
3. What is Andreia's feedback framework?
A Praise a strength first, then give one suggestion -- honest but kind.
B List every mistake first, then end abruptly.
C Only criticize, never praise.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Modals

GRAMMAR

Como dar feedback suave sobre algo que já aconteceu. / How to give soft feedback about something that already happened.

ModalForma / FormExemplo / Example
could havecould have + particicpio passadoYou could have explained it earlier.
should haveshould have + particicpio passadoWe should have tested it first.
would havewould have + particicpio passadoI would have asked for more time.
Negativashould not have + particicpioWe should not have rushed the report.

Atenção / Note: Os past modals falam de uma ação passada que não aconteceu. A forma é modal + have + particicpio passado (could have explained). Could have = uma escolha possível/melhor no passado; should have = o conselho certo no passado; would have = o que eu teria feito. Tornam o feedback suave e educado. Use have, nunca of (should have, não "should of"). / Past modals use modal + have + past participle to talk about a past action that did not happen.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"One of your is clear communication."
"May I make a small to improve it?"
"From my , the timing was the issue."
"Thank you. I your point and I will work on it."
"I will be but kind."
"Let us the delay before the next project."

Stage 2: Order the Feedback

Order

Listen first, then put the steps Andreia uses to give her feedback in the right order.

?Next, she softens the difficult part with a past modal: you could have explained the risk earlier.
?First, she praises a real strength: the team's clear communication.
?Finally, she invites their perspective and offers to address any questions.
?Then she shares her perspective and offers one suggestion.
?After that, she stays honest but kind, and acknowledges the team's effort.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

One of your strengths is clear communication.
Um dos seus pontos fortes é a comunicação clara.
You could have explained the risk earlier.
Você poderia ter explicado o risco mais cedo.
Thank you. I acknowledge your point and I will work on it.
Obrigada. Eu reconheço o seu ponto e vou trabalhar nisso.
I will be honest but kind: strengths first, then one suggestion.
Serei honesta mas gentil: pontos fortes primeiro, depois uma sugestão.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to soften feedback about a past action. Which is correct?
A "You could have explain the risk earlier."
B "You could have explained the risk earlier."
C "You could of explained the risk earlier."
You want to start the feedback the professional way. What do you do first?
A Praise a real strength before any suggestion.
B List every mistake immediately.
C Say nothing and let them guess.
Someone gives you feedback. How do you receive it well?
A "Thank you. I acknowledge your point and I will work on it."
B "You are wrong and you should not have said that."
C Ignore it and change the subject.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of one real piece of feedback you might give a colleague at work. In about two minutes, praise one strength first, then give one constructive suggestion using a past modal ("You could have..." or "We should have..."), share your perspective, stay honest but kind, and acknowledge their effort.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1One of your strengths is clear communication.Um dos seus pontos fortes é a comunicação clara.
2You could have explained the risk earlier.Você poderia ter explicado o risco mais cedo.
3May I make a small suggestion to improve it?Posso fazer uma pequena sugestão para melhorar?
4Thank you. I acknowledge your point and I will work on it.Obrigada. Eu reconheço o seu ponto e vou trabalhar nisso.
5I will be honest but kind: strengths first, then one suggestion.Serei honesta mas gentil: pontos fortes primeiro, depois uma sugestão.
Aula 20 -- Pre-class

Block 2 Review -- Leading a Supplier Meeting

Put it all together and chair a full supplier meeting from open to close, using the chair's words and all the Block 2 language. New vocabulary: chair, run through, take the floor, on the same page, show of hands, table, next steps, sign off, adjourn, takeaway.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Chair -- to lead a meeting: open it, guide the discussion, and keep it on time
"I will chair today's meeting. Let us begin." (Presidir / conduzir)
Run through -- to go over the agenda points quickly, one by one
"Let me run through the agenda before we start." (Revisar / passar por)
Take the floor -- to start speaking to the whole group, with everyone's attention
"Maria, would you like to take the floor?" (Tomar a palavra)
On the same page -- when everyone agrees and understands the same thing
"Good. I think we are all on the same page now." (Alinhados / de acordo)
Show of hands -- a quick, informal vote where people raise their hands
"Let us take a show of hands: who agrees with the plan?" (Votação de mãos)
Table -- to postpone an item and discuss it at a later meeting
"We are short on time, so let us table this point for next week." (Adiar)
Next steps -- the actions people will take after the meeting ends
"Before we close, let us agree on the next steps." (Próximos passos)
Sign off -- to give your final approval to a plan or decision
"Once everyone agrees, I will sign off on the budget." (Aprovar / dar o aval)
Adjourn -- to formally close a meeting, usually until a later date
"There is nothing else, so let us adjourn the meeting." (Encerrar)
Takeaway -- the most important point you remember from a meeting
"The main takeaway is that we approved the second console." (Ponto principal)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Chair
Run through
Take the floor
On the same page
Show of hands
Table
Next steps
Sign off
Adjourn
Takeaway

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Andreia will chair the supplier meeting. She opens and runs through the agenda so everyone is on the same page. A good chair uses all the Block 2 language at the right stage. To discuss options, she uses the second conditional: "If we ordered two consoles, we would save time." To give feedback on the past, she uses a past modal: "We could have planned this earlier." When it is time to decide, she takes a show of hands; if there is no time for one point, she will table it. Once everyone agrees, she will sign off on the plan. To close, she gives the main takeaway, agrees the next steps with a polite request -- "Could you send the summary?" -- and then she will adjourn the meeting.

1. Andreia says "If we ordered two consoles, we would save time." Which structure is this?
A The second conditional -- to discuss a possible option.
B A past modal -- to give feedback about the past.
C A simple order -- to tell people what to do.
2. There is no time for one agenda point. What does a good chair do?
A Adjourn the whole meeting at once.
B Table the point for a later meeting and keep on time.
C Sign off on it without any discussion.
3. How does Andreia close the meeting?
A She gives the takeaway, agrees the next steps with a polite request, and adjourns.
B She stops talking and leaves with no summary.
C She starts a brand-new agenda from the beginning.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Chair's Flow

GRAMMAR

Como conduzir uma reunião usando toda a linguagem do Bloco 2 em cada etapa. / How to chair a meeting using all the Block 2 language at each stage.

Etapa / StageEstrutura / StructureExemplo / Example
Open / Abrirpergunta / conviteShall we start? Let me run through the agenda.
Discuss / Discutirfirst / second conditionalIf we ordered two, we would save time.
Feedbackpast modal (could/should have)We could have planned it earlier.
Close / Fecharpedido educado + next stepsCould you send the next steps? Let us adjourn.

Atenção / Note: Um bom chair conduz a reunião em quatro etapas, usando a estrutura certa em cada uma: abrir com pergunta/convite; discutir opções com a primeira ou segunda condicional; dar feedback com past modal; e fechar com pedido educado e os próximos passos. Esta aula revisa TODA a linguagem do Bloco 2 numa única reunião real. / A good chair leads the meeting through four stages, using the right structure at each one.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I will today's meeting. Let me run through the agenda."
"Maria, would you like to take the ?"
"Let us take a show of : who agrees?"
"We are short on time, so let us this for next week."
"Once everyone agrees, I will sign on the plan."
"The main is the second console."

Stage 2: Order the Meeting

Order

Listen first, then put the steps Andreia uses to chair the meeting in the right order.

?Next, she takes a show of hands and signs off on the plan.
?First, she opens and runs through the agenda so everyone is on the same page.
?Finally, she sums up the takeaway, agrees the next steps, and adjourns the meeting.
?Then she invites people to take the floor and discusses the budget with the second conditional.
?After that, she tables the item there is no time for.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I will chair today's meeting. Let me run through the agenda.
Vou presidir a reunião de hoje. Deixe-me revisar a pauta.
Let us take a show of hands. Are we on the same page?
Vamos fazer uma votação de mãos. Estamos alinhados?
We are short on time, so let us table this for next week.
Estamos com pouco tempo, então vamos adiar isto para a próxima semana.
The main takeaway is clear. Could you send the next steps? Let us adjourn.
O ponto principal está claro. Poderia enviar os próximos passos? Vamos encerrar.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You start the meeting as the chair. What do you say?
A "We begin now. Talk."
B "Good morning. I will chair the meeting. Let me run through the agenda."
C "I have nothing to say today."
It is time to decide quickly with the group. What do you do?
A "Let us take a show of hands: who agrees with the plan?"
B "I will decide alone and tell you later."
C "Let us table the whole meeting."
The meeting is finished. How do you close it well?
A "The main takeaway is clear. Could you send the next steps? Let us adjourn."
B "Meeting over. Send the report."
C "Let us start the agenda again from item one."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you chair a short meeting at work. In about two minutes, open the meeting and run through three agenda points, discuss one option with the second conditional ("If we did X, we would..."), take one decision with a show of hands, and close with the main takeaway, the next steps, and adjourn.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1I will chair today's meeting. Let me run through the agenda.Vou presidir a reunião de hoje. Deixe-me revisar a pauta.
2Would you like to take the floor?Você gostaria de tomar a palavra?
3Let us take a show of hands. Are we on the same page?Vamos fazer uma votação de mãos. Estamos alinhados?
4We are short on time, so let us table this for next week.Estamos com pouco tempo, então vamos adiar isto para a próxima semana.
5The main takeaway is clear. Could you send the next steps? Let us adjourn.O ponto principal está claro. Poderia enviar os próximos passos? Vamos encerrar.

IN CLASS -- Select Lesson

01
Unlocking the English That's Already There
Diagnostic & Professional Identity -- 28 slides
02
My Career Story
From the ICU to Robotic Surgery -- 28 slides
03
What I Do Every Day
Routines & Responsibilities -- 28 slides
04
What's Happening Now
The Transition in Progress -- 28 slides
05
Talking About Experience
Have you ever...? Present perfect -- 28 slides
06
Connecting Past and Present
Present Perfect vs Past Simple -- 28 slides
07
Plans and Intentions
Future: going to vs will -- 28 slides
08
Describing People and Teams
Comparatives: describing your surgical team -- 28 slides
09
Asking Smart Questions in Meetings
Question forms in every tense: leading the meeting Q&A -- 28 slides
10
Block 1 Review -- My Professional Identity
All tenses together: your full professional self-presentation -- 28 slides
11
Opening a Meeting -- Agendas & Roles
Present simple and meeting frames: open the meeting, set the agenda, run the roles -- 28 slides
12
Talking Numbers -- Data, Volumes & Results
Present surgical data with confidence: numbers, quantifiers and the language of trends -- 28 slides
13
Asking For & Giving Updates
Report progress and status: say what is done, pending or behind, and ask colleagues for an update -- 28 slides
14
Solving Problems at Work
Describe a problem and suggest a solution: name the issue, the root cause and the impact, then propose a fix -- 28 slides
15
Reaching Agreement
Agree and disagree politely, then turn the discussion into a decision: action items, owners and deadlines -- 28 slides
16
Making Requests & Proposals
Ask for what you need politely and propose ideas the professional way -- Could I, Would you mind, I'd like to propose -- then put a proposal in writing -- 28 slides
17
Handling Problems & Complaints
Solve an issue on a call the professional way -- apologize, reassure, and offer a solution -- with the first conditional (If we ship today, it will arrive Monday) -- 28 slides
18
Negotiating Solutions
Make offers and trade-offs the professional way -- with the second conditional (If we had more time, we would test it) -- 28 slides
19
Giving & Receiving Feedback
Praise strengths and suggest improvements the professional way -- soften difficult messages with past modals (You could have explained it earlier) -- 28 slides
20
Block 2 Review -- Leading a Supplier Meeting
Put it all together -- chair a full supplier meeting from open to close with the chair's words and all the Block 2 language -- 28 slides

Materiais Complementares -- Aula 1

Lesson 1 — Unlocking the English That's Already There

TED Talk
The Power of Vulnerability -- Brené Brown

Sobre identidade, autenticidade e coragem para se mostrar -- ressoa com o seu objetivo de "ser você mesma em inglês". Inglês americano claro e emocional.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote 5 frases que falam sobre identidade.

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
The Medical Futurist Podcast -- Robotic Surgery Episodes

Contexto médico-tecnológico em inglês americano, com vocabulário de cirurgia robótica, dispositivos e inovação -- diretamente ligado à sua nova área.

Dica: ouça 15 minutos no caminho do trabalho e anote o vocabulário de medtech.

Find the podcast ↗

YouTube
Intuitive Surgical -- Da Vinci System Product Presentations

Apresentações reais do fabricante do Da Vinci em inglês. Ouça como profissionais descrevem o sistema robótico -- exatamente o tipo de reunião que você terá.

Dica: repita em voz alta as frases de apresentação do produto para treinar pronúncia.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — My Career Story

TED Talk
The Career Advice You Probably Didn't Get -- Susan Colantuono

Sobre trajetória e crescimento profissional. Ótimo modelo de como narrar uma carreira em inglês, no passado e no presente.

Dica: anote os verbos no passado que a palestrante usa para contar histórias.

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant -- Career Journeys

Episódios em que profissionais contam suas trajetórias. Inglês americano claro, cheio de past simple e storytelling de carreira.

Dica: ouça um episódio e anote 5 frases no passado para reusar na sua própria história.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Série
The Resident -- Season 1

Contexto hospitalar em inglês americano, com vocabulário médico naturalístico e personagens contando suas histórias profissionais.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e observe como os médicos descrevem casos no passado.

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Lesson 3 — What I Do Every Day

TED Talk
How to Gain Control of Your Free Time -- Laura Vanderkam

Sobre rotina, prioridades e organização do dia. Cheia de present simple e advérbios de frequência.

Dica: anote 5 frases com advérbios (always, usually, often).

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
The Daily (The New York Times)

Episódios de 20-25 min, inglês americano claro no presente. Ótimo para criar a rotina de ouvir inglês todo dia.

Dica: ouça um episódio por dia, sempre no mesmo horário.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
"A Day in the Life of a Surgical Nurse / Coordinator"

Profissionais descrevendo a rotina hospitalar em inglês, no presente simples.

Dica: repita em voz alta as frases de rotina do vídeo.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — What's Happening Now

TED Talk
The Surgeon of the Future / Robotic Surgery (Intuitive & TEDMED talks)

Cirurgia robótica em inglês americano, com muito present continuous descrevendo o que está acontecendo na sala. Diretamente ligado ao seu dia a dia.

Dica: anote frases no presente contínuo (is/are + -ing) que descrevem ações em andamento.

Watch on TED ↗

Podcast
The Medical Futurist Podcast -- Technology Rollouts

Episódios sobre implementação de novas tecnologias na saúde. Vocabulário de mudança (implement, roll out, pilot) em contexto real.

Dica: ouça e identifique 5 verbos de mudança usados no presente contínuo.

Find the podcast ↗

Série
Grey's Anatomy -- episódios de novos procedimentos

Inglês médico + dinâmica de equipe implementando novos métodos. Bom para ouvir present continuous em situações de mudança.

Dica: observe como descrevem o que está acontecendo na cirurgia em tempo real.

Disponível no streaming: Disney+

Lesson 6 — Connecting Past and Present

Series
The Good Doctor -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Burnt Food"

A surgical drama where doctors and nurses describe their training, their past cases, and what they have achieved so far. Perfect for hearing the past simple and the present perfect side by side in a hospital setting.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two sentences in the past simple (a finished moment) and two in the present perfect (an experience connected to now). Then describe your own career using the same pattern.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Globoplay e Prime Video

Podcast
The Nurse Keith Show -- "My Nursing Journey So Far"

Nurses look back on their careers and explain how they moved gradually from one role to another. Listen for since and for, and for time words like recently, lately, and throughout.

Note three sentences that connect the past to the present. Then record a short summary of your own journey, from the ICU to robotic surgery, using at least three new words from today.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Fun English Grammar Lesson: Past Simple vs Present Perfect - Learn the Difference (Examples + quiz)

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: when to use the past simple for finished times and the present perfect for experiences and actions that continue. Includes since vs for.

After watching, write five sentences about your career: connect what you did in the past with what you have achieved so far. Use ago, in 2016, since, for, and recently at least once each.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Plans and Intentions

Series
The Resident -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

A hospital drama where leaders launch new programs, set goals, and plan their next steps. Listen for how characters talk about the future: decided plans with going to and predictions or promises with will.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two sentences with going to (a decided plan) and two with will (a prediction or a promise). Then describe a plan of your own using the same pattern.

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "How to Run a Great Kickoff Meeting"

Leaders explain how they open a project: how they set a goal, build a roadmap, agree on deadlines, and forecast outcomes. Full of natural future language for the workplace.

Note three sentences that describe future plans. Then record a short kickoff for your own project, using at least three new words from today, such as goal, roadmap, and deadline.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
The Future in English: WILL vs. GOING TO

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: going to for decided plans and evidence-based predictions, and will for opinions, offers, promises, and on-the-spot decisions.

After watching, write five sentences about your next quarter at work: three plans with going to and two predictions or promises with will. Use deadline, goal, and outcome at least once each.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Describing People and Teams

Series
Grey's Anatomy -- Season 1, Episode 1: "A Hard Day's Night"

A surgical team where every member has different strengths: one is more skilled, one is calmer under pressure, one is more experienced. Listen for how the characters describe and compare each other.

Watch with English subtitles. Write three comparative sentences about the characters (more skilled than, calmer than, more confident than) and one superlative (the most reliable, the calmest). Then describe your own team the same way.

Disponível no streaming: Disney+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "What Makes a Great Team"

Leaders describe the people they work with: who is more reliable, who is more detail-oriented, who is the most supportive. Full of natural language for comparing colleagues at work.

Note three sentences that compare team members. Then record a short profile of two colleagues, using at least three new adjectives from today, such as reliable, supportive, and experienced.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Comparative & Superlative Adjectives - English Grammar Lesson (with PDF & Quiz)

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: short adjectives with -er than, long adjectives with more ... than, the spelling of -y adjectives, and the irregular forms better and worse.

After watching, write five sentences comparing the people on your team: three with -er than or more ... than, and two superlatives (the most ..., the best). Use skilled, calm, and reliable at least once each.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Asking Smart Questions in Meetings

Series
The Resident -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Doctors and nurses run fast meetings and ask sharp questions under pressure: What do we do now? Have you checked the chart? When did the symptoms start? Listen for how they ask questions in different tenses.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down five questions the characters ask and label each tense (present, past, present perfect, future). Then turn three of them into your own meeting questions.

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "The Art of Asking Better Questions"

The hosts explain how good questions move a meeting forward: how to clarify, follow up, bring up a concern, and ask for input. Full of natural language for taking part in a meeting.

Note three questions the speakers recommend. Then record yourself asking four meeting questions, using at least three new words from today, such as clarify, bring up, and feedback.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
100 Common English Questions and Answers | How to Ask and Answer Questions in English

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: the helper verbs do, did, have, and will, the word order of questions, and how to make polite, indirect questions.

After watching, write eight questions for a real meeting: two present simple, two past, two present perfect, and two future. Use clarify, concern, and input at least once each.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — Block 1 Review: My Professional Identity

Series
The Good Doctor -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Burnt Food"

A young surgeon introduces himself to a hospital board and presents his story: who he is, how he started, and what he wants to accomplish. Listen for how he uses different tenses to present his professional identity.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down five sentences he uses about himself and label the tense of each (present, past, present perfect, present continuous, future).

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Globoplay e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "How to Introduce Yourself with Confidence"

The hosts explain how to give a strong self-presentation: open with an overview, highlight your expertise and strengths, and end with your aspiration. Full of natural language for presenting your professional identity.

Note three tips for introducing yourself. Then record a two-minute self-presentation, using at least four new words from today, such as introduce, overview, expertise, and aspiration.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Tell me about yourself! Introduce yourself in English with EASE!

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: which tense to use for who you are, how you started, what you have done, what you are doing now, and where you are going.

After watching, record your three-minute self-presentation. Use one sentence in each tense, highlight one strength, and summarize your professional identity at the end.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — Opening a Meeting: Agendas & Roles

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 2, Episode 1: "The Dundies"

Watch how a manager opens a meeting: he welcomes everyone, sets the objective, and runs the agenda. A clear, sometimes funny model of meeting frames and the chairperson role.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three meeting frames the chair uses to open and run the meeting (for example, "Let's get started" or "Let's move on").

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "How to Run a Meeting People Don't Hate"

The hosts explain how a good chairperson opens a meeting: a clear objective, a short agenda, and the right roles. Full of natural language for setting up and running a meeting.

Note three tips for opening a meeting. Then record a one-minute meeting opening, using at least four new words from today, such as agenda, objective, item, and chairperson.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Important English Phrases to START & RUN Business Meetings

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's language: how to open a meeting, state the objective, introduce the agenda, move from item to item, and wrap up.

After watching, write a short meeting agenda for one of your real meetings: objective, attendees, three items with times, and who takes the minutes. Then open it out loud using the present simple.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Talking Numbers: Data, Volumes & Results

Series
Grey's Anatomy -- any episode with a department meeting

Notice how doctors report numbers: survival rates, the number of cases, and outcomes. A natural model for talking about figures, volumes and trends in a clinical setting.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three sentences that report a number or a rate (for example, "the survival rate is" or "we did roughly ten cases").

Disponível no streaming: Disney+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "Presenting Data So People Pay Attention"

The hosts explain how to present numbers clearly: give the big figure first, describe the trend, and round the numbers. Full of natural data language for a presentation.

Note three tips for presenting data. Then record a one-minute data summary, using at least four new words from today, such as figure, volume, rate, and trend.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Describe Trends in Business English - Describing Statistics, Sales and Market Trends

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's language: how to report figures, describe an increase or a decrease, use quantifiers, and talk about averages and percentages.

After watching, write a short data summary for one of your real reports: the total figure, the monthly volume, the rate, the trend, and your target. Then present it out loud using the present simple.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — Asking For & Giving Updates

Series
The Good Doctor -- any episode with a team check-in

Notice how the medical staff give quick status reports: what is done, what is still waiting, and what is behind. A natural model for asking for and giving updates in a clinical team.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three sentences that report a status, using just, already or yet (for example, "I have already prepped the patient" or "the results are not back yet").

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Globoplay e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "How to Run a Great Status Meeting"

The hosts explain how to give a clear project update: lead with the headline, separate what is complete from what is pending, and flag anything that is behind. Full of natural status language.

Note three tips for giving a clear update. Then record a one-minute status report on a real project, using at least four new words from today, such as update, milestone, pending, and on schedule.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
The present perfect with 'just', 'already' and 'yet' - 6 Minute Grammar

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: how to use just for something finished a moment ago, already for something done sooner than expected, and yet in negatives and questions.

After watching, write five status sentences about your own project: two with already, two with not ... yet, and one yet-question for a colleague. Then say them out loud.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Solving Problems at Work

Series
New Amsterdam -- any episode with a hospital crisis

Watch how Dr. Goodwin and his team face a problem, find the root cause and propose a workaround under pressure. A natural model for describing an issue and suggesting a solution in a clinical setting.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three suggestions the staff make, and notice the structures should, could and why don't we (for example, "We could move the patient" or "Why don't we call the specialist?").

Disponível no streaming: Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "How to Solve Problems Faster"

The hosts explain a simple way to describe a problem at work: name the issue, find the root cause, look at the impact, then propose a fix. Full of natural problem-solving language for meetings.

Note three tips for describing a problem clearly. Then record a one-minute brief on a real issue at your work, using at least four new words from today, such as issue, root cause, bottleneck and workaround.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Making Suggestions and Replies - let's, shall we, why don't we | English for Communication - ESL

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: how to suggest ideas politely with should for a strong recommendation, could for a softer option, and why don't we and what if to open a discussion.

After watching, write five suggestions for a problem at your work: two with should, two with could, and one with why don't we. Then say them out loud.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Reaching Agreement

Series
The Resident -- any episode with a board or team decision

Watch how the medical staff debate a plan, disagree politely and reach a compromise before they finalize a decision. A natural model for agreeing, pushing back gently, and assigning who does what.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two phrases people use to disagree politely (for example, "I see your point, but...") and one moment where someone assigns a task with a deadline.

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "How to Run a Meeting That Ends in a Decision"

The hosts explain how to move a group from discussion to a clear decision: build consensus, handle disagreement, reach a compromise, then assign action items with one owner and a deadline each.

Note three tips for closing a meeting. Then record a one-minute close on a real decision at your work, using at least four new words from today, such as consensus, compromise, action item and deadline.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
How to Agree and Disagree at Work: English Speaking Conversation Practice - UK & US

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's language: how to agree, how to disagree politely without sounding rude ("I see your point, but..."), and how to suggest a compromise to reach a decision.

After watching, write five sentences for a real meeting: one to agree, two to disagree politely, one to offer a compromise, and one to assign an action item with a deadline. Then say them out loud.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Making Requests & Proposals

Series
The Good Doctor -- any episode where a doctor pitches a treatment or new equipment

Watch how a doctor makes a case to the board or to a senior colleague: a polite request, a clear reason, the cost justified, and a request for approval. A natural model for proposing an idea at work.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two polite ways someone asks for something (for example, "Could I..." or "Would you mind...") and one sentence that justifies a cost or a decision.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Globoplay e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "How to Pitch an Idea to Your Boss"

The hosts explain how to propose an idea the professional way: open with a clear request, give the reason, justify the cost against a tight budget, and ask for approval with a deadline.

Note three tips for proposing an idea. Then record a one-minute spoken proposal for something you need at work, using at least four new words from today, such as proposal, budget, justify and approval.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Conversational English - How to make polite requests

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's language: how to ask for things politely with Could I and Could you, the very polite "Would you mind + -ing", and how to introduce a proposal.

After watching, write five sentences for a real situation at work: two polite requests, one with "Would you mind...", one proposal with "I would like to propose...", and one that asks for approval. Then say them out loud.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Handling Problems & Complaints

Series
The Good Doctor -- any episode where a team handles a complaint or a delayed procedure

Watch how staff handle a problem under pressure: they apologize, explain the issue, and offer a clear solution. A natural model for handling a complaint at work calmly.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two ways someone apologizes ("I apologize for..." / "Sorry for the inconvenience") and one first-conditional sentence (If..., we will...).

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Globoplay e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "How to Handle an Angry Customer or Colleague"

The hosts explain how to handle a complaint the professional way: apologize, stay calm, explain the problem, and offer a clear solution with a deadline.

Note three tips for handling a complaint. Then record a one-minute call for a real problem at work, using at least four new words such as complaint, apologize, solution and backup.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
First Conditional | Learn English Grammar about the First Conditional | Type 1 Conditionals

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's language: how to use the first conditional (If..., we will...) and how to apologize and offer a solution on a call.

After watching, write five sentences for a real situation at work: one apology, two first-conditional solutions (If..., we will...), one offer of help (I can / I will), and one that reassures the other person. Then say them out loud.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Negotiating Solutions

Series
Suits -- any episode where two sides reach a settlement

Watch how each side states a position, makes an offer, gives a concession, and asks for something in return until they reach a deal both sides can live with. A great model for trade-offs and counteroffers.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two offers and two trade-offs. Notice when a character says "If you did this, we would do that" -- that is the second conditional in action.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "How to Negotiate So Everyone Wins"

The hosts explain how to negotiate the professional way: state your position, make an offer, trade one concession for another, and aim for a mutual win instead of accepting the first number.

Note three tips for reaching a fair deal. Then record a one-minute negotiation opener using at least four new words from today, such as offer, concession, trade-off and mutual.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Second Conditional Sentences + Examples | English Grammar Lesson

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's language: how to make offers and trade-offs with the second conditional -- "If you gave us thirty days, we would sign today" -- and how to ask for a concession in return.

After watching, write five sentences for a real situation at work: two offers with the second conditional, one trade-off, one counteroffer, and one sentence that proposes a mutual agreement. Then say them out loud.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Giving & Receiving Feedback

Series
Ted Lasso -- any episode where the coach gives a player feedback

Watch how Ted praises a strength first, then gives one gentle suggestion to improve, staying honest but always kind. A natural model for feedback that helps instead of hurts.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down one moment of praise and one suggestion. Notice when a character softens the message with "you could have..." or "you should have..." -- those are past modals.

Disponível no streaming: Apple TV+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "How to Give and Receive Feedback Well"

The hosts explain a simple framework: praise a real strength first, then give one constructive suggestion, and acknowledge the other person's perspective -- honest but kind.

Note three tips for giving feedback. Then record a one-minute piece of feedback using at least four new words from today, such as constructive, strengths, suggestion and acknowledge.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Business English -- "Past Modals: should have, could have, would have"
