A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's language: how to soften feedback about the past with "You could have explained it earlier" and "We should have tested it first" -- modal + have + past participle.

After watching, write five feedback sentences for a real situation at work: one piece of praise, two suggestions with past modals, one sentence that acknowledges another perspective, and one that stays honest but kind. Then say them out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — Block 2 Review: Leading a Supplier Meeting

Series
The Office (US) -- any conference-room meeting episode

Watch how a meeting is opened, how the chair runs through the agenda, invites people to take the floor, and tries to reach a decision. A fun model of what a chair should -- and should not -- do.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two phrases the chair uses to open or close the meeting. Notice what happens when nobody is on the same page or when an item should have been tabled.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "How to Run a Meeting People Want to Attend"

The hosts explain how a good chair leads a meeting: open with a clear agenda, keep it on time, decide with the group, and close with clear next steps and one owner per task.

Note three tips for chairing a meeting. Then record a one-minute meeting opening and closing using at least four new words from today, such as chair, run through, next steps and takeaway.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Business English -- "How to Chair a Meeting in English"

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's language: how to open, run through the agenda, invite people to take the floor, take a show of hands, table an item, and close by agreeing the next steps and adjourning.

After watching, write five sentences for a real meeting at work: one to open, one to invite someone to take the floor, one to take a show of hands, one to table an item, and one to close with the next steps. Then say them out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Who You Are Key Words The Code In Context Practice Your Turn Wrap-Up
Lesson 1 -- Business English

Unlocking the English
That's Already There

B1 -- 60 minutes -- Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: Who You Are

Let's Talk About You

"In one word: what excites you most about robotic surgery?"
"Tell me about a normal day at Einstein."
Chapter 1: Who You Are

Your English Journey

"When do you need English the most in your new role?"
"What is the hardest part of speaking English in a meeting?"
Chapter 2: Key Words

Your Professional
Vocabulary

Eight essential words for professional identity

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

The experience and training that you have
Background
The experience and training that you have
"My background is in nursing."
Having the duty to manage or take care of something
Responsible For
Having the duty to manage or take care of something
"I'm responsible for the robotic surgery program."
To take action to handle a person, task, or situation
Deal With
To take action to handle a person, task, or situation
"I deal with suppliers like Medtronic and Stryker."
Having control or command of something
In Charge Of
Having control or command of something
"I'm in charge of coordinating the surgical team."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

To move from one role or stage to another
Transition Into
To move from one role or stage to another
"I'm transitioning into robotic surgery."
A company that provides products or services
Supplier
A company that provides products or services
"Medtronic is one of our main suppliers."
An operation performed using robotic systems
Robotic Surgery
An operation performed using robotic systems
"I'm transitioning into robotic surgery at Einstein."
A person with an interest or concern in a project
Stakeholder
A person with an interest or concern in a project
"The main stakeholders are the surgeons and the suppliers."
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Background"My background is in nursing."
Responsible For"I'm responsible for the program."
Deal With"I deal with suppliers."
In Charge Of"I'm in charge of the team."
Transition Into"I'm transitioning into robotic surgery."
Supplier"Medtronic is a supplier."
Robotic Surgery"I work with robotic surgery."
Stakeholder"The surgeons are stakeholders."
Chapter 3: The Code

Grammar Discovery

Look at the blue words. Now or finished?

I work at Einstein. I deal with suppliers every week.
Last year, I worked in the ICU and in neurology.
Yesterday, I met the Medtronic team.
I studied nursing for five years.
TenseUseExample
Present SimpleNow / routines / factsI work at Einstein.
Past SimpleFinished actions in the pastYesterday I worked with Medtronic.
QuestionsWh- + do/does/didWhat do you do? Who did you meet?
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"Yesterday I work with Medtronic."
"Yesterday I worked with Medtronic."

When the action is finished and in the past, use the past simple (-ed or irregular).

Practice

Fill in: Present or Past?

"I ___work at Hospital Albert Einstein."
"Last year, I ___transitioned into robotic surgery."
"I ___deal with suppliers like Medtronic every week."
"Yesterday, I ___met the Medtronic team."
"I ___studied nursing for five years."
"I am ___responsible for the robotic surgery program."
The Code

Asking Questions

Click to listen. These are the questions people ask in meetings.

Ask about someone's job.
"What do you do?"
Ask where someone is based.
"Where are you based?"
Ask about someone's responsibilities.
"What are you responsible for?"
Ask who they work with.
"Who do you deal with?"
Ask about a past meeting.
"Who did you meet yesterday?"
Chapter 4: In Context

Andreia on a Call
with Medtronic

Time to use your vocabulary in a real professional situation.

Artifact

Meeting Invitation

From: mark.collins@medtronic.com
To: andreia.heins@einstein.br
Subject: Robotic Surgery Program -- Kickoff Call

Dear Andreia,

Thank you for leading the new robotic surgery program at Hospital Albert Einstein. We would like to schedule a kickoff call with our team.

Topic: Da Vinci System -- Onboarding & Support

Date: March 12, 2026

Time: 10:00 AM (São Paulo) -- 30 minutes

Attendees: Andreia Heins (Einstein), Mark Collins (Medtronic), LATAM support team

Please confirm your availability. We look forward to working with you.

Mark Collins -- Medtronic LATAM

1. What is the topic of the call?
The Da Vinci System -- onboarding and support.
2. How long is the call?
30 minutes.
3. Who is the supplier in this email?
Medtronic (Mark Collins).
Listening

A Self-Introduction

Listen to a nurse introducing herself on a call.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What is the speaker's background?
Nursing.
2. What is she responsible for?
The robotic surgery program.
3. Which suppliers does she deal with?
Medtronic and Stryker.
Dialogue

Kickoff Call with Mark

M
Hi Andreia, thanks for joining. Could you introduce yourself?
A
Of course. My background is in nursing, and I currently work at Hospital Albert Einstein.
M
Great. What are you responsible for now?
A
I'm in charge of the robotic surgery program, and I deal with suppliers like Medtronic.
M
Perfect. So you're our main stakeholder for this project?
A
Exactly. I'm transitioning into robotic surgery, so I'll be your main contact.
M
Wonderful. Let's walk you through the system.
A
Sounds good. I'm ready to learn.
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. What does Mark ask Andreia to do at the start?
To introduce herself.
2. What does Mark call Andreia at the end of the call?
The main stakeholder / his main contact for the project.
3. What does Mark offer to do next?
To walk her through the system.
Listening

A Meeting Opener

Listen to the start of a supplier call.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What does Mark ask Andreia to do first?
To introduce herself and tell them about her role.
2. What else does he want to know?
Who she usually deals with on her team.
Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
A Medtronic rep asks: "What do you do?" Introduce yourself professionally.
"My background is in nursing. I'm in charge of the robotic surgery program at Einstein."
Detective

Spot the Error

"Yesterday I meet the Medtronic team."
"Yesterday I met the Medtronic team."
"I am responsible of the program."
"I am responsible for the program."
"She deal with international suppliers."
"She deals with international suppliers."
"Last year I work in the ICU."
"Last year I worked in the ICU."
"What you do at Einstein?"
"What do you do at Einstein?"
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Drilling

1. A supplier asks your name and role. What do you say?
"My name is Andreia. I'm in charge of the robotic surgery program at Einstein."
2. Someone asks what your background is. Respond.
"My background is in nursing. I worked in the ICU and in neurology."
3. A colleague asks who you deal with. Answer.
"I deal with international suppliers like Medtronic and Stryker."
4. Someone asks what you did yesterday. What do you say?
"Yesterday, I met the Medtronic team and discussed the new system."
5. You explain your career change. What do you say?
"I'm transitioning into robotic surgery, and I'm excited about this new chapter."
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Kickoff Call

You are on a kickoff call with Mark from Medtronic. Introduce yourself, explain your current role, and mention your transition into robotic surgery.
backgroundresponsible fordeal withtransition intosupplier
Role-Play

Role-Play: Meeting a Surgeon

A surgeon on your team asks about your background and your new role. Answer confidently. Use the past simple to talk about your career before robotic surgery.
backgroundin charge ofstakeholder
Free Practice

Role-Play: Congress Networking

You are at an international robotic surgery congress. A colleague from the LATAM region asks about your work. Have a real conversation. No keywords, no script. Just talk!

Use everything from today: your background, your current role, who you deal with, and your transition into robotic surgery.

Reflection

Record Yourself

Introduce yourself as if you are on a kickoff call with Medtronic. Include: your name, your background, your current role, and your transition into robotic surgery.

Click to start recording. Click again to stop.

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

Identity Badge Earned!

You can now introduce yourself in English -- as yourself, Andreia.

Survival Card

5 Key Phrases

1
"Hi, I'm Andreia. My background is in nursing."
2
"I currently work at Hospital Albert Einstein."
3
"I'm responsible for the robotic surgery program."
4
"I'm transitioning into surgical robotics."
5
"I deal with suppliers like Medtronic and Stryker."
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can introduce myself professionally in English.
I know 8 professional-identity words.
I can use the present simple for my current role.
I can use the past simple for my career history.
I can ask and answer questions in a meeting.
I can read and understand a meeting invitation.
I can talk about my transition into robotic surgery.
I feel more like myself when I speak English.
Lesson Complete

Day 1 -- Complete.

Next lesson: My Career Story -- From the ICU to Robotic Surgery

Lesson 2 -- Business English

My Career Story

B1 -- 60 minutes -- Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: Looking Back

Quick Recap

"Remind me: what is your background, and what are you responsible for now?"
"Which suppliers do you deal with?"
Chapter 1: Looking Back

Your Path

"Where did you study, and when did you graduate?"
"What was your first job in nursing?"
Chapter 2: Key Words

Words for Your
Career Story

Eight words to talk about your professional path

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

To finish your studies at a school or university
Graduate From
To finish your studies at a school or university
"I graduated from nursing school in 2008."
To focus on one particular area of work
Specialize In
To focus on one particular area of work
"I specialized in intensive care."
To move to a higher position at work
Get Promoted
To move to a higher position at work
"I got promoted to coordinator."
Something good that you did with effort
Achievement
Something good that you did with effort
"My biggest achievement was the robotic surgery program."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

A difficult task or situation
Challenge
A difficult task or situation
"The hardest challenge was learning a new system."
To be in front of and guide a team (past: led)
Lead
To be in front of and guide a team (past: led)
"I led a small team during the pandemic."
An important stage or event in your career
Milestone
An important stage or event in your career
"A key milestone was my move into robotic surgery."
A fixed period of working time, like the night shift
Shift
A fixed period of working time, like the night shift
"I worked the night shift for several years."
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Graduate From"I graduated from nursing school."
Specialize In"I specialized in intensive care."
Get Promoted"I got promoted to coordinator."
Achievement"My biggest achievement."
Challenge"The hardest challenge."
Lead (led)"I led a small team."
Milestone"A key milestone."
Shift"I worked the night shift."
Chapter 3: The Code

Grammar Discovery

All past. Regular (-ed) or irregular?

I graduated from nursing school in 2008.
I worked the night shift for six years.
I led a small team during the pandemic.
I got promoted to coordinator.
TypeFormExample
Regularverb + -edgraduate → graduated
Irregularspecial formlead → led · get → got
Time markersin 2008 / for / ago / whenI worked there for six years.
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I begin my career in the ICU."
"I began my career in the ICU."

Begin → began (irregular). A finished past action needs the past form.

Practice

Fill in: Past Simple

"I ___graduated from nursing school in 2008."
"I ___worked the night shift for six years."
"I ___led a small team during the pandemic."
"I ___got promoted to coordinator."
"Last year, I ___began my transition into surgery."
"I ___specialized in intensive care."
The Code

Irregular Past

Say the past form, then click to check.

lead → ?
led -- "I led a small team."
get → ?
got -- "I got promoted."
begin → ?
began -- "I began my transition."
become → ?
became -- "It became my biggest achievement."
make → ?
made -- "We made it work."
Chapter 4: In Context

Andreia's
Career Path

Your story, in English.

Artifact

Career Timeline

Andreia Heins -- Career Path -- Hospital Albert Einstein

2008 -- Graduated from nursing school.

2009-2015 -- Worked the night shift in the ICU; specialized in intensive care.

2016 -- Moved to neurology (administrative area).

2020 -- Led a small team during the pandemic and got promoted to coordinator.

2025 -- Began the transition into robotic surgery.

1. When did Andreia graduate?
In 2008.
2. What did she do during the pandemic?
She led a small team and got promoted to coordinator.
3. When did she begin robotic surgery?
In 2025 (last year).
Listening

A Career Story

Listen to a nurse describing her career path.

0:000:00
Speed
1. Where did she start her career?
In the ICU, on the night shift.
2. How did she become a coordinator?
She led a team during the pandemic and got promoted.
3. What was her biggest challenge?
Learning a new system for robotic surgery.
Dialogue

Telling Your Story to Dr. Alan

D
Andreia, I would love to hear your story. How did you start in nursing?
A
Well, I graduated from nursing school in 2008, and I started in the ICU.
D
The ICU is tough. What did you do there?
A
I worked the night shift for six years and I specialized in intensive care.
D
Impressive. And how did you move into robotic surgery?
A
I got promoted to coordinator, and last year I began my transition into surgery.
D
That is a big milestone. What was the hardest part?
A
The biggest challenge was learning a new system, but I led the team and we made it work.
Comprehension

About the Story

1. What did Andreia do for six years?
She worked the night shift in the ICU.
2. How did she become a coordinator?
She got promoted after leading a team during the pandemic.
3. What was the hardest part of the transition?
Learning a new system.
Listening

A Mentor's Questions

Listen to a mentor asking about your past.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What does the mentor ask about first?
Where and when she started her career.
2. What else does the mentor want to know?
Her biggest challenge and her proudest achievement.
Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
A colleague asks: "Where did you start your career?" Answer in the past.
"I started in the ICU, and I worked the night shift for six years."
Detective

Spot the Error

"I leaded a team during the pandemic."
"I led a team during the pandemic."
"I begin my career in the ICU."
"I began my career in the ICU."
"I work the night shift for six years."
"I worked the night shift for six years."
"I getted promoted to coordinator."
"I got promoted to coordinator."
"Where you did study?"
"Where did you study?"
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Drilling

1. A mentor asks where you studied. Answer.
"I graduated from nursing school in 2008."
2. Someone asks about your first job. Respond.
"I started in the ICU and worked the night shift for six years."
3. A colleague asks how you became a coordinator. Answer.
"I led a team during the pandemic and got promoted."
4. Someone asks about your biggest challenge. What do you say?
"The biggest challenge was learning a new system for robotic surgery."
5. Someone asks about your proudest achievement. Respond.
"My biggest achievement was opening the robotic surgery program."
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Congress Dinner

At a congress dinner, Dr. Alan asks about your career. Tell your story in the past: where you graduated, what you did, and how you got here.
graduatedworked the night shiftspecializedgot promotedled
Role-Play

Role-Play: Job Interview

A recruiter from another hospital asks about your past roles and one challenge you overcame. Answer in the past simple.
challengemilestoneachievement
Free Practice

Role-Play: Your Whole Story

A colleague from the LATAM region asks you to tell your whole career story. No keywords, no script. Just tell it, from 2008 to now.

Use the past simple: graduated, worked, specialized, led, got promoted, began.

Reflection

Record Yourself

Tell your career story in the past, from when you graduated to your move into robotic surgery. Include one challenge and one achievement.

Click to start recording. Click again to stop.

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

Storyteller Badge Earned!

You can now tell your career story in English -- in the past, Andreia.

Survival Card

5 Key Phrases

1
"I graduated from nursing school in 2008."
2
"I specialized in intensive care."
3
"I worked in the ICU for several years."
4
"I led a small team during the pandemic."
5
"Last year, I started my transition into robotic surgery."
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can tell my career story in the past.
I know 8 words to describe my career path.
I can use regular past verbs (-ed).
I can use irregular past verbs (led, got, began).
I can ask and answer past questions with "did".
I can read and understand a career timeline.
I can describe a challenge and an achievement.
I stopped using the present for the past.
Lesson Complete

Day 2 -- Complete.

Next lesson: What I Do Every Day -- Routines & Responsibilities

Lesson 3 -- Business English

What I Do Every Day

B1 -- 60 minutes -- Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: A Normal Day

From Past to Present

"Last time you told me your story. Now: what does a normal day look like for you?"
"What is the first thing you do at work?"
Chapter 1: A Normal Day

How Often?

"How often do you coordinate the surgical team?"
"What do you always do before a surgery?"
Chapter 2: Key Words

Words for Your
Daily Work

Eight words to talk about routines and responsibilities

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

The usual things you do regularly
Routine
The usual things you do regularly
"My routine starts at seven a.m."
A plan of when things happen
Schedule
A plan of when things happen
"I always check my schedule first."
To organize people or activities to work well together
Coordinate
To organize people or activities to work well together
"I coordinate the surgical team every day."
To watch and check something regularly
Monitor
To watch and check something regularly
"I monitor the equipment before surgery."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

To give information to a manager, or be managed by them
Report To
To give information to a manager, or be managed by them
"I report to the medical director."
A piece of work you have to do
Task
A piece of work you have to do
"My first task is the morning briefing."
The most important thing to do first
Priority
The most important thing to do first
"Safety is always my top priority."
New information about progress
Update
New information about progress
"I often give updates to the team."
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Routine"My routine starts at seven."
Schedule"I check my schedule."
Coordinate"I coordinate the team."
Monitor"I monitor the equipment."
Report To"I report to the director."
Task"My first task is the briefing."
Priority"Safety is my priority."
Update"I give updates to the team."
Chapter 3: The Code

Grammar Discovery

Where do the blue words go?

I always check my schedule in the morning.
I usually coordinate the surgical team.
I often monitor the equipment.
We never skip the safety check.
RulePatternExample
Before main verbsubject + adverb + verbI always check my schedule.
After "to be"subject + be + adverbSafety is always my priority.
Scalealways → usually → often → sometimes → never100% → 0%
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I check always my schedule."
"I always check my schedule."

Frequency adverbs go before the main verb (but after "to be").

Practice

Fill in: Present + Adverbs

"I ___always check my schedule first."
"She ___coordinates the surgical team."
"I ___usually monitor the equipment before surgery."
"Safety ___is always my priority."
"We ___never skip the safety check."
"I ___report to the medical director."
The Code

Frequency Scale

Say the adverb, then click to check.

100% → ?
always -- "I always check my schedule."
90% → ?
usually -- "I usually coordinate the team."
70% → ?
often -- "I often monitor the equipment."
40% → ?
sometimes -- "I sometimes deal with suppliers."
0% → ?
never -- "We never skip the safety check."
Chapter 4: In Context

Andreia's
Daily Schedule

Your day, in English.

Artifact

Daily Schedule

Andreia Heins -- A Typical Day -- Robotic Surgery Program

7:00 -- I always check my schedule and emails.

8:00 -- I coordinate the surgical team (morning briefing).

9:00 -- I monitor the equipment before the first surgery.

13:00 -- I sometimes deal with suppliers like Medtronic.

16:00 -- I report to the medical director and give updates.

1. What does she always do at 7:00?
She checks her schedule and emails.
2. When does she coordinate the team?
At 8:00, in the morning briefing.
3. Who does she report to at the end of the day?
The medical director.
Listening

A Daily Routine

Listen to a nurse describing her typical day.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What does she always do first?
She checks her schedule.
2. What does she do before every surgery?
She monitors the equipment.
3. What is her top priority?
Safety.
Dialogue

Onboarding Tom

T
Andreia, what does a normal day look like for you?
A
Well, my routine starts at seven. I always check my schedule first.
T
And then? What do you usually do?
A
I coordinate the surgical team and I monitor the equipment before every surgery.
T
Do you report to anyone?
A
Yes, I report to the medical director, and I often give her updates.
T
What is your top priority?
A
Priority? Safety is always my priority. We never skip the safety check.
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. What does Andreia always do first?
She checks her schedule.
2. Who does she report to?
The medical director.
3. What does the team never do?
They never skip the safety check.
Listening

A Colleague's Questions

Listen to a colleague asking about your routine.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What does the colleague ask about first?
What time her day starts and what she does first.
2. What else does the colleague want to know?
How often she coordinates the team and who she reports to.
Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
A new colleague asks: "What do you do first every day?" Answer with an adverb.
"I always check my schedule and emails first."
Detective

Spot the Error

"I check always my schedule."
"I always check my schedule."
"She coordinate the surgical team."
"She coordinates the surgical team."
"We skip never the safety check."
"We never skip the safety check."
"Safety always is my priority."
"Safety is always my priority."
"How often you coordinate the team?"
"How often do you coordinate the team?"
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Drilling

1. A colleague asks what you do first. Answer.
"I always check my schedule and emails first."
2. Someone asks how often you coordinate the team. Respond.
"I usually coordinate the surgical team every morning."
3. A new member asks who you report to. Answer.
"I report to the medical director, and I often give updates."
4. Someone asks about your top priority. What do you say?
"Safety is always my top priority. We never skip the safety check."
5. Someone asks what you do before surgery. Respond.
"I always monitor the equipment before every surgery."
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Onboarding

A new team member, Tom, asks you to explain your daily routine. Describe what you always, usually, and sometimes do, and your top priority.
routinealways checkcoordinatemonitorpriority
Role-Play

Role-Play: Process Audit

An auditor asks how you organize your day and how often you do each task. Answer in the present simple, using frequency adverbs.
scheduletaskreport toupdate
Free Practice

Role-Play: A Day in My Life

A colleague from the LATAM region asks you to walk them through a typical day, from morning to evening. No keywords, no script. Just describe it!

Use the present simple and frequency adverbs: always, usually, often, sometimes, never.

Reflection

Record Yourself

Describe your daily routine in the present simple. Include: what time you start, what you always do, what you usually do, and your top priority. Use frequency adverbs.

Click to start recording. Click again to stop.

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

Routine Badge Earned!

You can now describe your daily work in English, Andreia.

Survival Card

5 Key Phrases

1
"My routine starts at seven a.m."
2
"I always check my schedule first."
3
"I coordinate the surgical team every day."
4
"I report to the medical director."
5
"Safety is always my top priority."
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can describe my daily routine in English.
I know 8 words about routines and responsibilities.
I can use the present simple for my routine.
I put frequency adverbs in the right place.
I add -s for he/she/it.
I can read and understand a daily schedule.
I can say how often I do each task.
I can explain my responsibilities and priorities.
Lesson Complete

Day 3 -- Complete.

Next lesson: What's Happening Now -- The Transition in Progress

Lesson 4 -- Business English

What's Happening Now

B1 -- 60 minutes -- Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: Right Now

Routine vs Right Now

"What do you usually do at work? And what is different this month?"
"What is changing in the robotic surgery program right now?"
Chapter 1: Right Now

In Progress

"What are you learning at the moment?"
"What is the team working on these days?"
Chapter 2: Key Words

Words for
Change in Progress

Eight words to talk about your transition

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

To start using a new system or plan
Implement
To start using a new system or plan
"We are implementing the Da Vinci system."
To learn how to use something new
Train On
To learn how to use something new
"I am training on the new robot this month."
To introduce something new, step by step
Roll Out
To introduce something new, step by step
"We are rolling out the robotic surgery program."
To change in order to fit a new situation
Adapt To
To change in order to fit a new situation
"I am adapting to my new role."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

To prepare or install something new
Set Up
To prepare or install something new
"We are setting up the new lab."
Forward movement or development
Progress
Forward movement or development
"The program is making good progress."
A small test before the full launch
Pilot
A small test before the full launch
"We are running a pilot first."
To grow or become bigger
Expand
To grow or become bigger
"The team is expanding."
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Implement"We are implementing the system."
Train On"I am training on the robot."
Roll Out"We are rolling out the program."
Adapt To"I am adapting to my role."
Set Up"We are setting up the lab."
Progress"We are making progress."
Pilot"We are running a pilot."
Expand"The team is expanding."
Chapter 3: The Code

Grammar Discovery

Which is a routine, and which is happening now?

I usually work in the ICU.
This month, I am training on the new robot.
Right now, we are implementing the Da Vinci system.
These days, the program is expanding.
TenseUseExample
Present Simpleroutine / factI work in the ICU.
Present Continuousnow / temporary / changingI am training this month.
Signal wordsnow, right now, at the moment, these daysRight now, we are implementing...
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"At the moment, I adapt to the new role."
"At the moment, I am adapting to the new role."

For something happening now or temporarily, use the present continuous (am/is/are + -ing).

Practice

Fill in: Simple or Continuous?

"I usually ___work in the surgery department."
"This month, I ___am training on the new robot."
"Right now, we ___are implementing the Da Vinci system."
"Every morning, I ___check my schedule."
"These days, the program ___is expanding."
"At the moment, the team ___is adapting to the new system."
The Code

Routine or Now?

Say the correct form, then click to check.

Routine: every day → ?
"I check my schedule every day." (present simple)
Now: this month → ?
"I am training on the robot this month." (continuous)
Now: right now → ?
"We are implementing the system right now." (continuous)
Routine: usually → ?
"I usually coordinate the team." (present simple)
Now: these days → ?
"The program is expanding these days." (continuous)
Chapter 4: In Context

Project In Progress

What is happening right now.

Artifact

Status Update

Robotic Surgery Program -- Status Update -- Hospital Albert Einstein

In progress:

• We are implementing the Da Vinci system.

• The team is training on the new robot.

• We are running a pilot in one surgery room.

• We are setting up the new lab.

• The program is expanding to a second hospital.

1. What system are they implementing?
The Da Vinci system.
2. Where are they running the pilot?
In one surgery room.
3. What is expanding?
The program (to a second hospital).
Listening

A Status Update

Listen to a nurse describing what's happening now.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What is the team implementing?
The Da Vinci system.
2. What is she training on?
The new robot.
3. How is the project going?
It is making good progress and expanding.
Dialogue

Status Call with Brian

B
Hi Andreia, how is the rollout going right now?
A
It's going well. We are implementing the Da Vinci system this month.
B
Great. Are you training the team?
A
Yes, I am training on the new robot, and the team is adapting quickly.
B
Are you running a pilot first?
A
Exactly. We are running a pilot, and we are setting up the new lab.
B
And how is the project progressing?
A
It's making good progress. The program is expanding faster than we expected.
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. What is the team implementing this month?
The Da Vinci system.
2. What are they doing before the full launch?
They are running a pilot and setting up the new lab.
3. How is the program progressing?
It is expanding faster than expected.
Listening

A Manager's Questions

Listen to a manager asking about current progress.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What does the manager ask about first?
What she is working on right now.
2. What else does the manager want to know?
How the team is adapting and how the pilot is going.
Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
Brian asks: "What are you working on right now?" Answer in the present continuous.
"Right now, I am implementing the Da Vinci system and training the team."
Detective

Spot the Error

"Right now, I implement the new system."
"Right now, I am implementing the new system."
"We are implement the Da Vinci system."
"We are implementing the Da Vinci system."
"The team adapting to the new system."
"The team is adapting to the new system."
"Every day I am checking my schedule."
"Every day I check my schedule."
"What you are working on now?"
"What are you working on now?"
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Drilling

1. A manager asks what you are working on now. Answer.
"Right now, I am implementing the Da Vinci system."
2. Someone asks if you are training the team. Respond.
"Yes, I am training on the new robot, and the team is adapting quickly."
3. A colleague asks about the pilot. Answer.
"We are running a pilot in one surgery room first."
4. Someone asks how the project is going. Respond.
"It is making good progress, and the program is expanding."
5. Contrast your routine and now. What do you say?
"I usually work in surgery, but this month I am training on the new system."
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Status Call

Brian, the project manager, asks for a status update. Tell him what is happening right now: what you are implementing, training, and rolling out.
implementingtraining onrunning a pilotsetting upexpanding
Role-Play

Role-Play: Supplier Update

A Stryker supplier asks how the implementation is going. Describe what is in progress and how the team is adapting. Use the present continuous.
adaptingprogresspilot
Free Practice

Role-Play: My Transition Now

A colleague asks what your work is like these days. Contrast your usual routine with what you are doing right now in the transition. No keywords, no script. Just talk!

Use both: present simple (routine) and present continuous (now): "I usually... but right now I am..."

Reflection

Record Yourself

Describe what is happening in your work right now. Contrast your usual routine (present simple) with what you are doing these days (present continuous).

Click to start recording. Click again to stop.

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

Progress Badge Earned!

You can now talk about your change in progress, Andreia.

Survival Card

5 Key Phrases

1
"Right now, we are implementing the Da Vinci system."
2
"I am training on the new robot this month."
3
"We are rolling out the robotic surgery program."
4
"I am adapting to my new role."
5
"The program is making good progress."
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can talk about what is happening now at work.
I know 8 words about change and progress.
I can form the present continuous (am/is/are + -ing).
I know when to use present simple vs present continuous.
I can ask questions with "are you ...-ing?".
I can read and understand a project status update.
I can give a status update on my transition.
I can contrast my routine with what is changing now.
Lesson Complete

Day 4 -- Complete.

Next lesson: Talking About Experience -- Have You Ever?

Day 5 -- Talking About Experience

Have You Ever...?

Everything you have done — in one conversation

Warm-up

From Now to Ever

Last class you described the transition in progress. Let's remember:

"We are implementing the Da Vinci system."

"I am training the new team this month."

Now think:

That is what is happening now. But when Dr. Adams asks about your career — everything until today — which tense tells that story? "Have you ever assisted in a robotic procedure?"

Today's Goal

Own Your Experience

1. Ask

"Have you ever...?"

2. Answer

"I have... / I have never..."

3. Quantify

"more than fifty procedures"

Chapter 2

Expertise Words

8 words to present your experience

Expertise Words

Words 1-4

0 / 4 words revealed

A medical operation or formal process
Procedure
A medical operation or formal process
"I have assisted in fifty robotic procedures."
An official document that proves your skill
Certification
An official document that proves your skill
"I have completed two international certifications."
A safe practice of a real situation
Simulation
A safe practice of a real situation
"New nurses start with simulations."
An experienced person who guides you
Mentor
An experienced person who guides you
"Dr. Ferraz has been my mentor for years."
Expertise Words

Words 5-8

0 / 4 words revealed

Practical, doing the real work yourself
Hands-on
Practical, doing the real work yourself
"The training is very hands-on."
An important moment of progress in your story
Milestone
An important moment of progress in your story
"My first robotic case was a milestone."
To watch and guide other people's work
Supervise
To watch and guide other people's work
"I have supervised many simulation sessions."
The most modern and advanced of its kind
Cutting-edge
The most modern and advanced of its kind
"Robotic surgery is cutting-edge technology."
Pronunciation Drill

Say It Clearly

Procedure
Certification
Milestone
Cutting-edge
Chapter 3

The Career Tense

Have you ever...? · I have... · I have never...

Discover the Rule

Listen and Notice

"Have you ever assisted in a robotic procedure?"

"Yes, I have. I have assisted in more than fifty."

"I have completed two certifications."

"I have never operated the console alone."

StructureUseExample
Have you ever + past participle?Ask about the whole careerHave you ever supervised a team?
I have + past participle (+ number)Your experience, quantifiedI have completed two certifications.
I have never + past participleNot once until nowI have never presented abroad.

No specific date — your whole career until now. Numbers make it powerful: "more than fifty procedures".

Common Mistake

Right vs Wrong

✗ WRONG

"I have worked there in 2019."

✓ RIGHT

"I worked there in 2019." (data = past simple)

✗ WRONG

"Have you ever assist in a surgery?"

✓ RIGHT

"Have you ever assisted in a surgery?"

Grammar Practice

Complete the Sentence

"Have you _____ worked with the Da Vinci console?" [show]

"I have _____ in more than fifty procedures." (helped during) [show]

"I have _____ presented my work abroad." (not once) [show]

"She has _____ many simulation sessions." (watched and guided) [show]

"My first robotic case was a real _____." (important progress moment) [show]

Chapter 4

The Visit

Dr. Adams arrives on Friday

Artifact

Your Experience Log

HOSPITAL ALBERT EINSTEIN · ROBOTIC SURGERY

Experience Log — Andreia Heins

Robotic procedures assisted50+
International certifications2
Simulation hours supervised120
Presentations abroad0 — yet!

Mentor: Dr. Ferraz · Program: Da Vinci

Listening

Dr. Adams' Message

Listen to the voice message about Friday's visit.

0:000:00
Speed

1. Where does Dr. Adams work?

Click to reveal

2. What does he want to talk about?

Click to reveal

Listening 2

The Model Summary

0:000:00
Speed

1. How many procedures has she assisted in?

Click to reveal

2. What has she never done?

Click to reveal

3. What is her biggest milestone?

Click to reveal

The Visit

Dr. Adams at Einstein

D
Andreia, your hospital is impressive. Have you ever worked with the newest Da Vinci console?
A
Yes, I have. I have assisted in more than fifty robotic procedures.
D
Fifty! And have you ever supervised a simulation session?
A
Many times. The training is very hands-on — I have supervised about one hundred and twenty hours.
D
Excellent. Have you ever presented your work abroad?
A
No, I have never presented abroad. But I have completed two international certifications.
D
That is a real milestone. We need people like you in our exchange program.
A
Thank you, Dr. Adams! Working with cutting-edge technology is my passion. It would be an honor.
Comprehension

About Dr. Adams

What does Dr. Adams think of the hospital?

Click to reveal

What does he ask about?

Click to reveal

What does he offer at the end?

Click to reveal

Chapter 5

Quick Fire

6 situations -- Answer fast!

Quick Fire

Situation Challenge

1. Ask a visiting surgeon about robotic surgery experience.

Show Answer

2. Present your numbers: 50+ procedures.

Show Answer

3. Say what you have never done (present abroad) — and keep it positive.

Show Answer

4. Talk about your certifications.

Show Answer

5. Describe the training style at Einstein.

Show Answer

6. Say your first robotic case was an important moment.

Show Answer

Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"Have you ever assist in a surgery?"

Click to correct

"I have completed the course in 2023."

Click to correct

"She have supervised many sessions."

Click to correct

"I never have operated the console."

Click to correct

Oral Drilling

Repeat After the Audio

"Have you ever assisted in a robotic procedure?"

"I have assisted in more than fifty."

"I have never presented abroad."

"My first robotic case was a real milestone."

Error Correction

Let's Fix These

Errors from today's class:

Chapter 6

Your Turn

Guided > Semi-free > Free

Role-Play: Guided

The Interview

Scenario: Dr. Adams asks about your career: procedures, simulations, certifications. Answer with your real numbers.

Keyword chips:

I have assisted in... I have completed... I have never...
Role-Play: Semi-free

You Interview Him

Scenario: Now YOU interview Dr. Adams about robotic surgery in Chicago: ask three "Have you ever...?" questions about his career.

Keywords:

Have you ever trained...? Have you ever worked with...?
Role-Play: Free

The Exchange Pitch

Scenario: Present your full experience to the exchange program committee: your procedures, your training hours, your certifications, your milestones — and the one thing you have never done, but want to do.

No keywords. Own your story!

Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can ask "Have you ever...?" about professional experience.
I can present my experience with numbers: "I have assisted in more than fifty procedures."
I can say what I have never done — and stay positive ("yet!").
I know when to switch to past simple: specific dates.
I know the words: procedure, certification, simulation, mentor, hands-on, milestone, supervise, cutting-edge.
Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

Expertise Badge Earned!

Your experience speaks English now, Andreia.

Day 5 -- Complete.

Next lesson: Connecting Past and Present

LESSON 1
T
